nodes.brief:
Develops the arithmetic of number fields and their completions: ideal class groups, unit groups, and Galois theory over global fields, the p-adic and local-field analogues, and number-theoretic properties of finite fields and related commutative rings. Class field theory and local-global principles give this area a mature, largely settled core, even as programs such as Iwasawa theory keep parts of it in active development.

### Concepts
- **Algebraic number theory: global fields** (11Rxx) — Studies the arithmetic of number fields, including ideal class groups, unit groups, and Galois theory over global fields.
- **Algebraic number theory: local and p-adic fields** (11Sxx) — Studies local and p-adic fields, their extensions, ramification theory, and p-adic analysis.
- **Finite fields and commutative rings (number-theoretic aspects)** (11Txx) — Studies number-theoretic aspects of finite fields and related commutative rings, including polynomial factorization and character sums over them.

nodes.name: Computational, Logical and Applied Number Theory
nodes.en: Gathers the computational, logical, and application-facing corners of number theory: decidability and model-theoretic questions such as Hilbert's tenth problem, algorithms for factorization, primality testing, and other effective computations, and the application of number-theoretic results to fields such as cryptography and coding theory. The computational and applied threads are highly active given cryptographic demand, while the logical connections to model theory and decidability remain a narrower, more specialized niche.
nodes.cs: Sdružuje výpočetní, logické a aplikačně orientované oblasti teorie čísel: otázky rozhodnutelnosti a teorie modelů, jako je Hilbertův desátý problém, algoritmy pro faktorizaci, testování prvočíselnosti a další efektivní výpočty, a aplikace teoreticko-číselných výsledků v oborech jako kryptografie a teorie kódování. Výpočetní a aplikované směry jsou vzhledem ke kryptografické poptávce velmi aktivní, zatímco logické souvislosti s teorií modelů a rozhodnutelností zůstávají užší, specializovanější oblastí.
nodes.brief:
Gathers the computational, logical, and application-facing corners of number theory: decidability and model-theoretic questions such as Hilbert's tenth problem, algorithms for factorization, primality testing, and other effective computations, and miscellaneous applications of number-theoretic results to fields such as cryptography and coding theory. The computational and applied threads are highly active given cryptographic demand, while the logical connections remain a narr

### Concepts
- **Connections of number theory and logic** (11Uxx) — Studies model-theoretic and decidability questions arising in number theory, such as Hilbert's tenth problem.
- **Computational number theory** (11Yxx) — Develops and analyzes algorithms for factorization, primality testing, and other explicit number-theoretic computations.
- **Miscellaneous applications of number theory** (11Zxx) — Covers applications of number-theoretic results outside pure number theory, such as in cryptography and coding theory.

nodes.name: Field theory and polynomials
nodes.en: Branch of abstract algebra studying fields — commutative structures with addition, multiplication, and division — and the polynomials defined over them, culminating in Galois theory's correspondence between field extensions and their symmetry groups. Extends into topological, differential, and generalized (non-commutative) field structures, and underlies algebraic number theory and algebraic geometry wherever a base field's arithmetic must be made explicit.
nodes.cs: Odvětví abstraktní algebry zabývající se tělesy — komutativními strukturami se sčítáním, násobením a dělením — a polynomy nad nimi, vrcholící Galoisovou teorií, která dává do souvislosti rozšíření těles s jejich grupami symetrií. Zahrnuje také topologické, diferenciální a zobecněné (nekomutativní) tělesové struktury a je základem algebraické teorie čísel a algebraické geometrie všude tam, kde je třeba explicitně popsat aritmetiku výchozího tělesa.
nodes.brief:
Fields are the commutative structures where division is always possible, and this pillar studies them together with the polynomials defined over them. Its crown is Galois theory, the correspondence between field extensions and their symmetry groups that turns the solvability of equations into the group theory of [[02.01.04.16]]. Topological, differential and non-commutative generalizations extend the classical theory's reach.

It is upstream infrastructure for much of algebra and arithmetic: number fields feed [[02.01.04.07]], function fields and polynomial rings feed [[02.01.04.09]] and [[02.01.04.10]], finite fields supply the alphabets of the coding theory in [[02.01.04.51]], and skew fields (division rings) mark the border with the associative rings of [[02.01.04.12]].

nodes.name: Classical Field Theory and Extensions
nodes.en: Covers the core structural theory of fields: the real and complex fields as prototypical examples, the general axiomatic theory of fields, and the theory of field extensions crowned by Galois theory. Galois cohomology extends these extension-theoretic results into a homological framework, linking field theory to group cohomology and the Brauer group. This block forms the classical, textbook-settled backbone of the discipline, underlying algebraic number theory, algebraic geometry, and coding theory.
nodes.cs: Zahrnuje jádro strukturální teorie těles: reálná a komplexní tělesa jako prototypické příklady, obecnou axiomatickou teorii těles a teorii rozšíření těles vrcholící Galoisovou teorií. Galoisova kohomologie rozšiřuje tyto výsledky teorie rozšíření do homologického rámce a propojuje teorii těles s grupovou kohomologií a Brauerovou grupou. Tento blok tvoří klasické, učebnicově ustálené jádro oboru, na němž stojí algebraická teorie čísel, algebraická geometrie a teorie kódování.
nodes.brief:
Covers the core structural theory of fields: the real and complex fields as prototypical examples, the general axiomatic theory of fields, and the theory of field extensions crowned by Galois theory. Galois cohomology extends these extension-theoretic results into a homological framework, linking field theory to group cohomology and the Brauer group. This block forms the classical, textbook-settled backbone of the discipline, underlying algebraic number theory, algebraic geom

### Concepts
- **Real and complex fields** (12Dxx) — Studies the algebraic, order-theoretic, and analytic structure of the real and complex number fields, real-closed fields, and their formally real generalizations.
- **General field theory** (12Exx) — Develops the foundational algebraic theory of fields, including finite fields, polynomial equations over fields, and structural results independent of a particular extension.
- **Field extensions** (12Fxx) — Studies algebraic, transcendental, separable, and normal field extensions and their Galois groups via the fundamental theorem of Galois theory.
- **Homological methods** (12Gxx) — Applies cohomological tools, especially Galois cohomology and Brauer group theory, to classify and obstruct field extensions and central simple algebras.

nodes.name: Extended, Topological, and Generalized Field Structures
nodes.en: Groups the subclasses that enrich or relax the field axioms, or place field theory in dialogue with adjacent disciplines. Differential and difference algebra endow fields with extra operators to develop a Galois theory of differential and difference equations; topological fields add a compatible topology, yielding valued and local fields; generalizations of fields loosen commutativity or invertibility axioms (skew fields, near-fields); and the logic connection studies fields as structures in the model-theoretic sense.
nodes.cs: Sdružuje podtřídy, které obohacují nebo uvolňují axiomy těles, případně staví teorii těles do dialogu s příbuznými disciplínami. Diferenciální a diferenční algebra vybavuje tělesa dalšími operátory a rozvíjí Galoisovu teorii diferenciálních a diferenčních rovnic; topologická tělesa přidávají kompatibilní topologii a dávají vzniknout valuovaným a lokálním tělesům; zobecnění těles uvolňují axiomy komutativity nebo invertibility — kosá tělesa (skew fields) a near-tělesa (near-fields); logická vazba pak zkoumá tělesa jako struktury v modelově-teoretickém smyslu.
nodes.brief:
Groups the subclasses that enrich or relax the field axioms, or place field theory in dialogue with adjacent disciplines. Differential and difference algebra endow fields with extra operators to develop a Galois theory of differential and difference equations; topological fields add a compatible topology, yielding valued and local fields; generalizations of fields loosen commutativity or invertibility axioms (skew fields, near-fields); and the logic connection studies fields 

### Concepts
- **Differential and difference algebra** (12Hxx) — Studies fields and rings equipped with a derivation or difference operator, extending Galois-theoretic methods to differential and difference equations.
- **Topological fields** (12Jxx) — Studies fields endowed with a compatible topology, including valued fields, local fields, and their completions.
- **Generalizations of fields** (12Kxx) — Studies near-fields, skew fields, and other algebraic structures obtained by relaxing one or more field axioms.
- **Connections between field theory and logic** (12Lxx) — Studies the model theory of fields, including quantifier elimination, definability, and decidability of first-order field theories.

nodes.name: Commutative algebra
nodes.en: Studies commutative rings and their ideals, modules, and localizations — the algebraic backbone of algebraic geometry and algebraic number theory, since coordinate rings of varieties and rings of integers are both commutative rings. Spans elementary ring and module theory, homological techniques (free resolutions, Tor, Ext), distinguished ring classes (Dedekind domains, Noetherian rings), and computational tools such as Gröbner bases.
nodes.cs: Zabývá se komutativními okruhy a jejich ideály, moduly a lokalizacemi — algebraickým základem algebraické geometrie a algebraické teorie čísel, neboť souřadnicové okruhy variet i okruhy celých čísel jsou komutativní okruhy. Zahrnuje elementární teorii okruhů a modulů, homologické metody (volné rezoluce, Tor, Ext), vyznačené třídy okruhů (Dedekindovy obory, noetherovské okruhy) a výpočetní nástroje jako Gröbnerovy báze.
nodes.brief:
Commutative algebra studies commutative rings, their ideals and their modules — the coordinate rings of algebraic varieties and the rings of integers of number fields both live here, which makes the pillar the shared algebraic engine of [[02.01.04.10]] algebraic geometry and [[02.01.04.07]] number theory. Localization, Noetherian conditions, and distinguished ring classes such as Dedekind domains organize the structural theory.

Its homological methods — free resolutions, Ext and Tor, depth and dimension — are the concrete workhorse of the abstract machinery in [[02.01.04.14]], and its computational side, Gröbner bases and elimination, powers the computer-algebra practice of [[02.01.03]]. Field-theoretic foundations come from [[02.01.04.08]]; dropping commutativity leads to [[02.01.04.12]].

nodes.name: Structural Foundations of Commutative Rings
nodes.en: Covers the basic vocabulary of commutative algebra: general ring theory, ring extensions, and local/semilocal rings. These subclasses supply the elementary structural building blocks — ideals, spectra, localization, integral extensions, and local behavior — on which the rest of the discipline is built.
nodes.cs: Zahrnuje základní pojmový aparát komutativní algebry: obecnou teorii okruhů, rozšíření okruhů a lokální a semilokální okruhy. Tyto podtřídy poskytují elementární strukturální stavební kameny — ideály, spektra, lokalizaci, celistvá rozšíření a lokální chování — na nichž stojí zbytek oboru.
nodes.brief:
Covers the basic vocabulary of commutative algebra: general ring theory, ring extensions, and local/semilocal rings. These subclasses supply the elementary structural building blocks — ideals, spectra, localization, integral extensions, and local behavior — on which the rest of the discipline is built.

### Concepts
- **General commutative ring theory** (13Axx) — Studies the elementary structure of commutative rings — ideals, quotients, localization, and prime spectra — as the foundational vocabulary of commutative algebra.
- **Commutative ring extensions and related topics** (13Bxx) — Examines extensions of commutative rings, including integral, flat, and faithfully flat extensions, and how ring-theoretic properties behave under base change and descent.
- **Local rings and semilocal rings** (13Hxx) — Develops the theory of rings with a unique (or finitely many) maximal ideal, including regular local rings and multiplicities, underlying the local structure of algebraic geometry and singularity theory.

nodes.name: Modules, Ideals, and Homological Methods
nodes.en: Treats the theory of modules and ideals over commutative rings alongside the homological machinery — free resolutions, Tor and Ext, depth, regular sequences — and the chain and finiteness conditions (Noetherian, Artinian) that constrain and organize them. This cluster forms the technical engine of modern commutative algebra, underpinning dimension theory and intersection theory in algebraic geometry.
nodes.cs: Pojednává o teorii modulů a ideálů nad komutativními okruhy spolu s homologickým aparátem — volnými rezolucemi, funktory Tor a Ext, hloubkou a regulárními posloupnostmi — a řetězcovými a konečnostními podmínkami (noetherovské, artinovské), které je omezují a strukturují. Tento shluk tvoří technický motor moderní komutativní algebry a stojí v základu teorie dimenze a teorie průniku v algebraické geometrii.
nodes.brief:
Treats the theory of modules and ideals over commutative rings alongside the homological machinery — free resolutions, Tor and Ext, depth, regular sequences — and the chain and finiteness conditions (Noetherian, Artinian) that constrain and organize them. This cluster forms the technical engine of modern commutative algebra, underpinning dimension theory and intersection theory in algebraic geometry.

### Concepts
- **Theory of modules and ideals in commutative rings** (13Cxx) — Studies modules over commutative rings and the ideals structuring them, including projective, injective, and flat modules, syzygies, and ideal-theoretic invariants.
- **Homological methods in commutative ring theory** (13Dxx) — Applies homological algebra — free resolutions, Tor, Ext, depth, regular sequences — to commutative rings, yielding invariants such as projective dimension, Cohen-Macaulay, and Gorenstein properties.
- **Chain conditions, finiteness conditions in commutative ring theory** (13Exx) — Studies Noetherian, Artinian, and other finiteness or chain conditions on rings and modules that guarantee well-behaved decomposition, primary decomposition, and dimension theory.

nodes.name: Special and Distinguished Classes of Commutative Rings
nodes.en: Classifies commutative rings singled out by arithmetic, factorization, or cardinality constraints: arithmetic rings such as Dedekind and Prüfer domains, integral domains with their factorization theory, and finite commutative rings. Each class carries its own toolkit of invariants, with applications spanning algebraic number theory, valuation theory, and coding theory.
nodes.cs: Klasifikuje komutativní okruhy vyčleněné aritmetickými, faktorizačními nebo mohutnostními omezeními: aritmetické okruhy jako Dedekindovy a Prüferovy obory, obory integrity s jejich faktorizační teorií a konečné komutativní okruhy. Každá třída má vlastní sadu invariantů s aplikacemi sahajícími od algebraické teorie čísel přes teorii valuací až po teorii kódování.
nodes.brief:
Classifies commutative rings singled out by arithmetic, factorization, or cardinality constraints: arithmetic rings such as Dedekind and Prüfer domains, integral domains with their factorization theory, and finite commutative rings. Each class carries its own toolkit of invariants, with applications spanning algebraic number theory, valuation theory, and coding theory.

### Concepts
- **Arithmetic rings and other special commutative rings** (13Fxx) — Studies rings distinguished by arithmetic properties of their ideals, such as Dedekind domains, Prüfer rings, valuation rings, and unique factorization domains.
- **Integral domains** (13Gxx) — Investigates commutative rings without zero divisors, their divisibility and factorization theory, and classification into UFDs, PIDs, GCD domains, and Krull domains.
- **Finite commutative rings** (13Mxx) — Studies commutative rings with finitely many elements, their structure and classification, with applications to coding theory and combinatorics.

nodes.name: Topological, Logical, Differential, and Computational Methods
nodes.en: Gathers approaches that import structure or techniques from other fields into commutative algebra: topologies on rings and modules (adic and valuation topologies, completions), model-theoretic and logical methods, differential structures (derivations, differential ideals), and algorithmic techniques such as Gröbner bases. These subclasses mark commutative algebra's expanding interfaces with analysis, mathematical logic, differential equations, and symbolic computation.
nodes.cs: Sdružuje přístupy, které do komutativní algebry přejímají struktury nebo techniky z jiných oborů: topologie na okruzích a modulech (adické a valuační topologie, zúplnění), teoreticko-modelové a logické metody, diferenciální struktury (derivace, diferenciální ideály) a algoritmické techniky jako Gröbnerovy báze. Tyto podtřídy vyznačují rozšiřující se rozhraní komutativní algebry s analýzou, matematickou logikou, diferenciálními rovnicemi a symbolickými výpočty.
nodes.brief:
Gathers approaches that import structure or techniques from other fields into commutative algebra: topologies on rings and modules (adic and valuation topologies, completions), model-theoretic and logical methods, differential structures (derivations, differential ideals), and algorithmic techniques such as Gröbner bases. These subclasses mark commutative algebra's expanding interfaces with analysis, mathematical logic, differential equations, and symbolic computation.

### Concepts
- **Topological rings and modules** (13Jxx) — Studies commutative rings and modules equipped with a compatible topology, such as adic or valuation topologies, and their completions.
- **Applications of logic to commutative algebra** (13Lxx) — Applies model theory and other branches of mathematical logic to study commutative rings, their elementary theories, and definability of ideals and elements.
- **Differential algebra** (13Nxx) — Studies commutative rings equipped with derivations, differential ideals, and differential modules, forming the algebraic backbone of differential Galois theory and the theory of differential equations.
- **Computational aspects and applications of commutative rings** (13Pxx) — Develops algorithms — Gröbner bases, elimination theory, computer algebra systems — for computing with ideals, modules, and rings, with applications throughout algebraic geometry and beyond.

nodes.name: Algebraic geometry
nodes.en: Studies geometric objects — varieties and schemes — defined as solution sets of polynomial equations, using tools from commutative algebra, topology, and complex analysis to classify their shape and structure. Spans scheme-theoretic foundations, moduli spaces and cohomology of families, birational classification, curves and abelian varieties, arithmetic and real forms, and enumerative and computational techniques.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá geometrické objekty — variety a schémata — definované jako množiny řešení polynomiálních rovnic a pomocí nástrojů komutativní algebry, topologie a komplexní analýzy klasifikuje jejich tvar a strukturu. Zahrnuje základy teorie schémat, moduli prostory a kohomologii rodin, biracionální klasifikaci, křivky a abelovské variety, aritmetické a reálné formy a enumerativní a výpočetní techniky.
nodes.brief:
Algebraic geometry studies the shapes cut out by polynomial equations — varieties and schemes — with tools drawn from the commutative algebra of [[02.01.04.09]], the topology of [[02.01.04.41]], and the several-variable complex analysis of [[02.01.04.22]]. Scheme theory, cohomology, moduli spaces and birational classification form its core; curves and abelian varieties are its most classical objects.

Over non-closed fields it fuses with number theory as the arithmetic geometry shared with [[02.01.04.07]]; its enumerative branch — Gromov-Witten theory and mirror symmetry — is in standing dialogue with the string-theoretic side of [[01.01]]; and its tropical and computational corners trade with [[02.01.04.04]] and the computer algebra of [[02.01.03]].

nodes.name: Foundations, Local Theory and Cycles
nodes.en: Covers the scheme-theoretic and sheaf-theoretic foundations on which the rest of algebraic geometry is built, the local structure of varieties and schemes near singular or special points, and the theory of algebraic cycles and subschemes that generalizes divisors to higher codimension. Intersection theory, Chow groups, and deformation theory of singularities sit at the technical core of this block, feeding directly into moduli theory and the study of motives.
nodes.cs: Zahrnuje teorii schémat a svazků, na níž stojí zbytek algebraické geometrie, lokální strukturu variet a schémat v okolí singulárních nebo speciálních bodů a teorii algebraických cyklů a podschémat, která zobecňuje divizory do vyšší kodimenze. Technickým jádrem tohoto bloku je teorie průniku, Chowovy grupy a deformační teorie singularit, jež přímo napájejí teorii moduli a studium motivů.
nodes.brief:
Covers the scheme-theoretic and sheaf-theoretic foundations on which the rest of algebraic geometry is built, the local structure of varieties and schemes near singular or special points, and the theory of algebraic cycles and subschemes that generalizes divisors to higher codimension. Intersection theory, Chow groups, and deformation theory of singularities sit at the technical core of this block, feeding directly into moduli theory and the study of motives.

### Concepts
- **Foundations of algebraic geometry** (14Axx) — Establishes the scheme-theoretic, sheaf-theoretic, and categorical language (schemes, stacks, morphisms) underlying modern algebraic geometry.
- **Local theory in algebraic geometry** (14Bxx) — Studies the local structure of varieties and schemes, including deformation theory, singularities, and formal/local moduli.
- **Cycles and subschemes** (14Cxx) — Develops the theory of algebraic cycles, Chow groups, and intersection theory on varieties and schemes.

nodes.name: Families, Fibrations and Cohomology Theories
nodes.en: Concerns varieties that move in families - fibrations, degenerations, and moduli spaces - together with the cohomological invariants (etale, de Rham, crystalline, and motivic) used to detect and classify them. These tools translate geometric variation into linear-algebraic and Galois-theoretic data, underpinning Hodge theory and the theory of motives.
nodes.cs: Zabývá se varietami pohybujícími se v rodinách — fibracemi, degeneracemi a moduli prostory — spolu s kohomologickými invarianty (étalovou, de Rhamovou, krystalickou a motivickou kohomologií) používanými k jejich rozpoznání a klasifikaci. Tyto nástroje převádějí geometrickou variaci na lineárně-algebraická a Galoisovsko-teoretická data a stojí v základu Hodgeovy teorie a teorie motivů.
nodes.brief:
Concerns varieties that move in families - fibrations, degenerations, and moduli spaces - together with the cohomological invariants (etale, de Rham, crystalline, and motivic) used to detect and classify them. These tools translate geometric variation into linear-algebraic and Galois-theoretic data, underpinning Hodge theory and the theory of motives.

### Concepts
- **Families, fibrations in algebraic geometry** (14Dxx) — Studies families of varieties, their degenerations, variation of Hodge structure, and moduli of fibered spaces.
- **(Co)homology theory in algebraic geometry** (14Fxx) — Studies etale, de Rham, crystalline, and motivic (co)homology theories and their sheaf-theoretic foundations for varieties.

nodes.name: Birational Geometry and Classification of Varieties
nodes.en: Addresses the classification of algebraic varieties up to birational equivalence, including the minimal model program, rationality questions, and the finer taxonomy of surfaces, higher-dimensional varieties, and distinguished special classes such as toric, Fano, and Calabi-Yau varieties. This is the geometric core of the Mori-Enriques-Kodaira classification program.
nodes.cs: Zabývá se klasifikací algebraických variet až na biracionální ekvivalenci, včetně programu minimálních modelů, otázek racionality a jemnější taxonomie ploch, vícedimenzionálních variet a vyznačených speciálních tříd, jako jsou torické, Fanovy a Calabi-Yauovy variety. Jde o geometrické jádro Moriho-Enriquesova-Kodairova klasifikačního programu.
nodes.brief:
Addresses the classification of algebraic varieties up to birational equivalence, including the minimal model program, rationality questions, and the finer taxonomy of surfaces, higher-dimensional varieties, and distinguished special classes such as toric, Fano, and Calabi-Yau varieties. This is the geometric core of the Mori-Enriques-Kodaira classification program.

### Concepts
- **Birational geometry** (14Exx) — Studies varieties up to birational equivalence via blow-ups, the minimal model program, and rationality problems.
- **Surfaces and higher-dimensional varieties** (14Jxx) — Classifies algebraic surfaces and higher-dimensional varieties, extending Enriques-Kodaira-type classification schemes.
- **Special varieties** (14Mxx) — Studies distinguished classes of varieties - toric, determinantal, Grassmannian, complete-intersection, and flag varieties - with rich extra structure.

nodes.name: Curves, Abelian Varieties and Algebraic Groups
nodes.en: Treats the three classical families of varieties carrying group or near-group structure: algebraic curves and their moduli, abelian varieties as projective group varieties, and algebraic groups more generally. This is one of the most mature areas of algebraic geometry, with deep links to number theory, representation theory, and complex analysis via Jacobians and period maps.
nodes.cs: Pojednává o třech klasických rodinách variet nesoucích grupovou nebo téměř-grupovou strukturu: algebraických křivkách a jejich moduli, abelovských varietách jako projektivních grupových varietách a obecněji algebraických grupách. Jde o jednu z nejvyzrálejších oblastí algebraické geometrie s hlubokými vazbami na teorii čísel, teorii reprezentací a komplexní analýzu prostřednictvím Jacobiho variet a periodových zobrazení.
nodes.brief:
Treats the three classical families of varieties carrying group or near-group structure: algebraic curves and their moduli, abelian varieties as projective group varieties, and algebraic groups more generally. This is one of the most mature areas of algebraic geometry, with deep links to number theory, representation theory, and complex analysis via Jacobians and period maps.

### Concepts
- **Curves in algebraic geometry** (14Hxx) — Studies algebraic curves, their moduli spaces, Jacobians, and the geometry of linear systems on curves.
- **Abelian varieties and schemes** (14Kxx) — Studies abelian varieties and abelian schemes as projective algebraic group varieties, including polarizations and isogenies.
- **Algebraic groups** (14Lxx) — Studies algebraic groups and group schemes as geometric objects, complementing their linear and Lie-theoretic treatments.

nodes.name: Arithmetic and Real Algebraic Geometry
nodes.en: Studies algebraic varieties over non-algebraically-closed base fields, split between Diophantine and arithmetic geometry over number fields and finite fields, and real algebraic geometry over the reals with its semialgebraic and o-minimal flavor. Rational-point problems, heights, and Galois representations make this a central bridge to number theory.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá algebraické variety nad tělesy, jež nejsou algebraicky uzavřená: na jedné straně diofantickou a aritmetickou geometrii nad číselnými a konečnými tělesy, na druhé reálnou algebraickou geometrii nad reálnými čísly s jejím semialgebraickým a o-minimálním rázem. Problémy racionálních bodů, výšky a Galoisovy reprezentace z ní činí ústřední most k teorii čísel.
nodes.brief:
Studies algebraic varieties over non-algebraically-closed base fields, split between Diophantine and arithmetic geometry over number fields and finite fields, and real algebraic geometry over the reals with its semialgebraic and o-minimal flavor. Rational-point problems, heights, and Galois representations make this a central bridge to number theory.

### Concepts
- **Arithmetic problems in algebraic geometry; Diophantine geometry** (14Gxx) — Studies rational and integral points, heights, and Galois representations attached to varieties over arithmetic fields.
- **Real algebraic and real-analytic geometry** (14Pxx) — Studies varieties and semialgebraic sets defined over the real numbers and their real-analytic counterparts.

nodes.name: Enumerative, Computational, Affine and Tropical Geometry
nodes.en: Groups the more combinatorial and algorithmic corners of algebraic geometry: counting geometric configurations via intersection and Gromov-Witten theory, computing with varieties via effective algebraic algorithms, studying affine varieties and their embeddings, and tropicalizing varieties into piecewise-linear combinatorial shadows. These areas trade heavily with combinatorics, computer algebra, and mirror symmetry.
nodes.cs: Sdružuje kombinatoričtější a algoritmičtější kouty algebraické geometrie: počítání geometrických konfigurací pomocí průnikové a Gromovovy–Wittenovy teorie, efektivní algebraické algoritmy pro výpočty s varietami, studium afinních variet a jejich vnoření a tropikalizaci variet do po částech lineárních kombinatorických stínů. Tyto oblasti si intenzivně vyměňují podněty s kombinatorikou, počítačovou algebrou a zrcadlovou symetrií.
nodes.brief:
Groups the more combinatorial and algorithmic corners of algebraic geometry: counting geometric configurations via intersection and Gromov-Witten theory, computing with varieties via effective algebraic algorithms, studying affine varieties and their embeddings, and tropicalizing varieties into piecewise-linear combinatorial shadows. These areas trade heavily with combinatorics, computer algebra, and mirror symmetry.

### Concepts
- **Projective and enumerative algebraic geometry** (14Nxx) — Studies intersection numbers, Gromov-Witten invariants, and Schubert calculus for counting geometric configurations.
- **Computational aspects in algebraic geometry** (14Qxx) — Develops algorithms, Grobner-basis methods, and effective computation for varieties and their invariants.
- **Affine geometry** (14Rxx) — Studies affine varieties, embeddings of affine space, and problems such as the Jacobian conjecture.
- **Tropical geometry** (14Txx) — Studies tropicalizations of varieties as piecewise-linear combinatorial shadows encoding their algebraic structure.

nodes.name: Linear and multilinear algebra; matrix theory
nodes.en: Linear and multilinear algebra and matrix theory studies vector spaces, linear transformations, and their matrix representations, together with multilinear constructions such as tensor and exterior algebras and the classification of matrices by structural or spectral properties. It supplies the computational and structural bedrock for numerical analysis, statistics, geometry, and representation theory, distinct from the ring- and field-theoretic branches of algebra alongside it.
nodes.cs: Lineární a multilineární algebra a teorie matic zkoumá vektorové prostory, lineární zobrazení a jejich maticové reprezentace spolu s multilineárními konstrukcemi, jako jsou tenzorové a vnější algebry, a klasifikací matic podle strukturálních či spektrálních vlastností. Poskytuje výpočetní a strukturální základ pro numerickou analýzu, statistiku, geometrii a teorii reprezentací a odlišuje se tak od okolních okruhových větví algebry a teorie těles.
nodes.brief:
Linear algebra is mathematics' most exported product: vector spaces, linear maps and their matrix representations, extended to the tensor and exterior algebras of multilinear theory and to the classification of matrices by structural or spectral properties. It is deliberately the concrete, computation-ready layer — the ring- and field-theoretic abstractions sit next door in [[02.01.04.12]] and [[02.01.04.08]].

Downstream, matrix factorizations and spectral theory are the daily bread of numerical analysis in [[02.01.04.46]], of covariance structure in [[02.01.04.45]], of the representation theory of groups in [[02.01.04.16]], and of the state-space models of [[02.01.04.50]]; carried to infinite dimensions, linear algebra becomes the functional analysis of [[02.01.04.34]].

nodes.name: Foundations of Linear Algebra
nodes.en: Foundations of Linear Algebra treats vector spaces, linear transformations, matrices, determinants, eigenvalues, and canonical forms, together with multilinear constructions such as tensor products, symmetric and exterior powers, and Clifford algebras. It is the settled algebraic bedrock underlying nearly every quantitative discipline, from numerical analysis to representation theory and geometry.
nodes.cs: Základy lineární algebry se zabývají vektorovými prostory, lineárními zobrazeními, maticemi, determinanty, vlastními čísly a kanonickými tvary spolu s multilineárními konstrukcemi, jako jsou tenzorové součiny, symetrické a vnější mocniny a Cliffordovy algebry. Tvoří ustálený algebraický základ téměř každé kvantitativní disciplíny, od numerické analýzy po teorii reprezentací a geometrii.
nodes.brief:
Covers the core structural theory of vector spaces, linear transformations, matrices, determinants, eigenvalues, canonical forms, and multilinear constructions such as tensor and exterior algebras. This is the settled algebraic bedrock underlying nearly every quantitative discipline, from numerical analysis to representation theory. It also includes multilinear algebra topics like tensor products, symmetric and exterior powers, and Clifford algebras insofar as they arise from

### Concepts
- **Basic linear algebra** (15Axx) — Studies vector spaces, linear maps, matrices, determinants, eigenvalues/eigenvectors, canonical forms, and multilinear operations such as tensor, symmetric, and exterior products.

nodes.name: Special Matrices and Their Properties
nodes.en: Classifies matrices by structural or spectral properties — symmetric, Hermitian, orthogonal, positive definite, nonnegative, sparse, structured (Toeplitz, Hankel, circulant), and other distinguished families — and studies the inequalities, factorizations, and combinatorial or order-theoretic properties specific to each class. Such classifications underpin numerical stability analysis, combinatorial matrix theory, and applications across statistics, optimization, and control.
nodes.cs: Třídí matice podle strukturních či spektrálních vlastností — symetrické, hermitovské, ortogonální, pozitivně definitní, nezáporné, řídké, strukturované (Toeplitzovy, Hankelovy, cirkulantní) a další význačné rodiny — a zkoumá nerovnosti, rozklady a kombinatorické či uspořádáním dané vlastnosti specifické pro každou třídu. Tato třídění jsou oporou analýzy numerické stability, kombinatorické teorie matic a aplikací ve statistice, optimalizaci a řízení.
nodes.brief:
Classifies matrices by structural or spectral properties — symmetric, Hermitian, orthogonal, positive definite, nonnegative, sparse, structured (Toeplitz, Hankel, circulant), and other distinguished families — and studies the inequalities, factorizations, and combinatorial or order-theoretic properties specific to each class. Such classifications underpin numerical stability analysis, combinatorial matrix theory, and applications across statistics, optimization, and control. 

### Concepts
- **Special matrices** (15Bxx) — Studies matrices distinguished by structural, spectral, sign, or combinatorial properties, including symmetric, positive definite, nonnegative, orthogonal, and patterned (e.g. Toeplitz, circulant) matrices, together with their associated inequalities and factorizations.

nodes.name: Associative rings and algebras
nodes.en: Associative rings and algebras studies rings and algebras whose multiplication is associative but not necessarily commutative, covering structure and radical theory, modules and their homological and representation-theoretic invariants, ring constructions such as group rings and skew-polynomial extensions, polynomial identities, and Hopf algebras and quantum groups. It complements commutative algebra, which specializes to the commutative case, and nonassociative algebra, which drops associativity altogether.
nodes.cs: Asociativní okruhy a algebry zkoumají okruhy a algebry, jejichž násobení je asociativní, ale obecně nekomutativní, a zahrnují strukturální a radikálovou teorii, moduly s jejich homologickými a reprezentačně-teoretickými invarianty, konstrukce okruhů jako grupové okruhy a šikmé polynomiální rozšíření, polynomiální identity a Hopfovy algebry a kvantové grupy. Doplňují komutativní algebru, která se specializuje na komutativní případ, a neasociativní algebru, jež asociativitu zcela opouští.
nodes.brief:
Here multiplication is associative but not necessarily commutative: division rings, matrix rings, group rings, and the algebras of operators and quantum symmetry. Structure theory (Wedderburn-Artin, radicals), module and representation theory with its homological invariants, ring constructions, polynomial identities, and Hopf algebras with quantum groups make up its strata.

The pillar sits between its two siblings — the commutative specialization [[02.01.04.09]] and the nonassociative generalization [[02.01.04.13]] — while its group rings tie it to [[02.01.04.16]] and its homological and categorical machinery to [[02.01.04.14]]. Quantum groups keep it in contact with the low-dimensional topology of [[02.01.04.42]] and the quantum-symmetry side of [[01.01]].

nodes.name: Foundations, Radicals and Structure Theory
nodes.en: Covers the classical backbone of noncommutative ring theory: the general theory of associative rings, the Wedderburn-Artin classification of semisimple Artinian rings and division rings, local and semiperfect rings, and the radical theory (Jacobson, nil, prime) that measures how far a ring departs from semisimplicity. These results form the settled structural vocabulary that every other area of the discipline builds on.
nodes.cs: Pokrývá klasickou páteř nekomutativní teorie okruhů: obecnou teorii asociativních okruhů, Wedderburnovu–Artinovu klasifikaci polojednoduchých artinovských okruhů a okruhů s dělením, lokální a semiperfektní okruhy a teorii radikálů (Jacobsonův radikál, nilradikál, prvoradikál), která měří, jak daleko má okruh k polojednoduchosti. Tyto výsledky tvoří ustálený strukturní slovník, na němž staví všechny ostatní oblasti disciplíny.
nodes.brief:
Covers the classical backbone of noncommutative ring theory: the general theory of associative rings, the Wedderburn-Artin classification of semisimple Artinian rings and division rings, local and semiperfect rings, and the radical theory (Jacobson, nil, prime) that measures how far a ring departs from semisimplicity. These results form the settled structural vocabulary that every other area of the discipline builds on.

### Concepts
- **General and miscellaneous** (16Bxx) — Covers foundational notions and cross-cutting results in associative ring theory not assigned to a more specific subclass.
- **Division rings and semisimple Artin rings** (16Kxx) — Studies rings in which every nonzero element is invertible and Artinian rings with zero Jacobson radical, classified by the Wedderburn-Artin theorem.
- **Local rings and generalizations** (16Lxx) — Studies associative rings with a unique maximal one-sided ideal and their generalizations, such as semiperfect and semilocal rings.
- **Radicals and radical properties of associative rings** (16Nxx) — Studies the Jacobson, nil, prime, and other radical ideals that control a ring's departure from semisimplicity and organize its structure theory.

nodes.name: Modules, Homological Methods and Representation Theory
nodes.en: Treats associative algebras through the modules, bimodules, and ideals acting on them, the homological invariants (projective and injective dimension, Ext, Tor, Hochschild cohomology) attached to those modules, and the representation theory that classifies modules via quivers and Auslander-Reiten theory. Tilting theory, derived and cluster categories keep this area under active development well beyond its classical roots.
nodes.cs: Pojednává o asociativních algebrách skrze moduly, bimoduly a ideály, jež na nich působí, skrze homologické invarianty (projektivní a injektivní dimenze, Ext, Tor, Hochschildova kohomologie) přiřazené těmto modulům a skrze teorii reprezentací, která moduly klasifikuje pomocí toulců a teorie Auslandera a Reitenové. Teorie tiltingu a derivované a klastrové kategorie udržují tuto oblast v aktivním vývoji daleko za jejími klasickými kořeny.
nodes.brief:
Treats associative algebras through the modules, bimodules, and ideals acting on them, the homological invariants (projective and injective dimension, Ext, Tor, Hochschild cohomology) attached to those modules, and the representation theory that classifies modules via quivers and Auslander-Reiten theory. Tilting theory, derived and cluster categories keep this area under active development well beyond its classical roots.

### Concepts
- **Modules, bimodules and ideals in associative algebras** (16Dxx) — Studies one-sided and two-sided modules, bimodules, and the lattice of ideals governing associative algebras.
- **Homological methods in associative algebras** (16Exx) — Applies homological algebra — projective and injective dimension, Ext, Tor, Hochschild and cyclic cohomology — to associative rings and algebras.
- **Representation theory of associative rings and algebras** (16Gxx) — Classifies modules over associative algebras using quivers, Auslander-Reiten theory, and related tools for describing indecomposable representations.

nodes.name: Orders, Ring Constructions and Additional Structure
nodes.en: Groups finite-dimensional algebras and their arithmetic orders together with the standard machinery for building new associative rings — group rings, crossed products, Ore and skew-polynomial extensions — and with rings enriched by extra structure such as gradings, filtrations, and involutions. The blend of arithmetic, constructive, and structural techniques keeps parts of this area, notably graded and involutive constructions, in active research.
nodes.cs: Sdružuje konečněrozměrné algebry a jejich aritmetické řády se standardním aparátem pro budování nových asociativních okruhů — grupovými okruhy, zkříženými součiny, Oreho rozšířeními a okruhy šikmých polynomů — a s okruhy obohacenými o další strukturu, jako jsou gradace, filtrace a involuce. Směs aritmetických, konstruktivních a strukturních technik udržuje části této oblasti, zejména gradované a involutivní konstrukce, v živém výzkumu.
nodes.brief:
Groups finite-dimensional algebras and their arithmetic orders together with the standard machinery for building new associative rings — group rings, crossed products, Ore and skew-polynomial extensions — and with rings enriched by extra structure such as gradings, filtrations, and involutions. The blend of arithmetic, constructive, and structural techniques keeps parts of this area, notably graded and involutive constructions, in active research.

### Concepts
- **Associative algebras and orders** (16Hxx) — Studies finite-dimensional associative algebras and orders inside them, linking algebraic structure to arithmetic properties.
- **Associative rings and algebras arising under various constructions** (16Sxx) — Studies rings produced by standard constructions such as group rings, crossed products, and Ore or skew-polynomial extensions.
- **Associative rings and algebras with additional structure** (16Wxx) — Studies associative rings equipped with extra structure such as gradings, filtrations, involutions, or group actions.

nodes.name: Polynomial Identities, Chain Conditions and Element Properties
nodes.en: Polynomial Identities, Chain Conditions and Element Properties studies rings governed by finiteness or growth restrictions such as the Noetherian and Artinian chain conditions, rings satisfying a polynomial identity — a broad generalization of commutativity that encompasses all finite-dimensional algebras — and rings characterized by element-level properties such as nilpotence, idempotence, or regularity. Kemer's resolution of the Specht problem and the classical chain-condition theory leave the area largely settled, with PI-theoretic questions remaining the main active front.
nodes.cs: Polynomiální identity, řetězové podmínky a vlastnosti prvků zkoumají okruhy podléhající podmínkám konečnosti nebo růstu, jako jsou noetherovská a artinovská řetězová podmínka, okruhy splňující polynomiální identitu — široké zobecnění komutativity zahrnující všechny konečněrozměrné algebry — a okruhy charakterizované vlastnostmi jednotlivých prvků, jako je nilpotence, idempotence nebo regularita. Kemerovo řešení Spechtova problému a klasická teorie řetězových podmínek činí tuto oblast převážně uzavřenou, přičemž hlavní aktivní frontu tvoří otázky PI-teorie.
nodes.brief:
Covers rings governed by finiteness or growth restrictions (Noetherian, Artinian, and related chain conditions), rings satisfying a polynomial identity — a broad generalization of commutativity encompassing all finite-dimensional algebras — and rings characterized by properties of individual elements such as nilpotence, idempotence, or regularity. Kemer's resolution of the Specht problem and the classical chain-condition theory leave this area largely settled, with PI-theoret

### Concepts
- **Chain conditions, growth conditions, and other forms of finiteness** (16Pxx) — Studies Noetherian, Artinian, and related growth or finiteness conditions imposed on associative rings and algebras.
- **Rings with polynomial identity** (16Rxx) — Studies rings satisfying a nontrivial polynomial identity, a class generalizing commutative rings and all finite-dimensional algebras.
- **Conditions on elements** (16Uxx) — Studies rings defined by properties of their elements, such as nilpotents, idempotents, units, and von Neumann regular elements.

nodes.name: Hopf Algebras, Generalizations and Computational Aspects
nodes.en: Covers Hopf algebras and quantum groups — bialgebras with an antipode that generalize group symmetry and underpin quantum topology and noncommutative geometry — together with structures generalizing associative rings, such as near-rings and semirings, and the algorithms and software used to compute with associative rings. Quantum group theory remains a vigorous research frontier tied to representation theory, mathematical physics, and low-dimensional topology.
nodes.cs: Pokrývá Hopfovy algebry a kvantové grupy — bialgebry s antipodem, jež zobecňují grupovou symetrii a nesou kvantovou topologii a nekomutativní geometrii — spolu se strukturami zobecňujícími asociativní okruhy, jako jsou skorookruhy a polookruhy, a s algoritmy a softwarem pro výpočty s asociativními okruhy. Teorie kvantových grup zůstává živou výzkumnou frontou spjatou s teorií reprezentací, matematickou fyzikou a nízkodimenzionální topologií.
nodes.brief:
Covers Hopf algebras and quantum groups — bialgebras with an antipode that generalize group symmetry and underpin quantum topology and noncommutative geometry — together with structures generalizing associative rings, such as near-rings and semirings, and the algorithms and software used to compute with associative rings. Quantum group theory remains a vigorous research frontier tied to representation theory, mathematical physics, and low-dimensional topology.

### Concepts
- **Hopf algebras, quantum groups and related topics** (16Txx) — Studies Hopf algebras and quantum groups, bialgebras with a coalgebra structure and antipode that generalize group symmetry.
- **Generalizations** (16Yxx) — Studies structures generalizing associative rings, such as near-rings and semirings, that relax ring axioms.
- **Computational aspects of associative rings** (16Zxx) — Studies algorithms, complexity, and software for computing with associative rings and algebras.

nodes.name: Nonassociative rings and algebras
nodes.en: Nonassociative rings and algebras studies algebraic systems whose multiplication drops the associativity axiom, replacing it with weaker identities such as alternativity, power-associativity, the Jacobi identity, or the Jordan identity. The branch is dominated by Lie algebras, arising from infinitesimal symmetry, and Jordan algebras, arising from quantum-mechanical observables, alongside more general alternative and power-associative constructions.
nodes.cs: Neasociativní okruhy a algebry zkoumají algebraické systémy, jejichž násobení neplní axiom asociativity a je nahrazeno slabšími identitami, jako je alternativnost, mocninná asociativita, Jacobiho identita nebo Jordanova identita. Této větvi dominují Lieovy algebry, vznikající z infinitezimální symetrie, a Jordanovy algebry, vznikající z kvantově-mechanických pozorovatelných veličin, spolu s obecnějšími alternativními a mocninně-asociativními konstrukcemi.
nodes.brief:
Dropping associativity opens the door to the most physically consequential algebras in mathematics: Lie algebras, the infinitesimal symmetries of the Lie groups in [[02.01.04.17]], and Jordan algebras, born from the observables of quantum mechanics in [[01.01]]. Weaker identities — alternativity, power-associativity, the Jacobi and Jordan identities — replace the associative law, with the octonions as the emblematic alternative example.

The representation theory of semisimple Lie algebras, with its root systems and highest-weight classification, is the pillar's crown jewel and the backbone of symmetry analysis across mathematics and physics. The associative theory it generalizes remains next door in [[02.01.04.12]], connected through universal enveloping algebras.

nodes.name: General Nonassociative Rings
nodes.en: General Nonassociative Rings studies rings and algebras without the associativity axiom in full generality, developing the structural vocabulary — ideals, radicals, nilpotency, power-associativity, alternativity — that specializes into the Lie, Jordan, and other named subtheories. It provides the unifying framework of identities, varieties of algebras, and universal enveloping constructions against which more specific nonassociative classes are measured, with alternative algebras such as the octonions as a classical example.
nodes.cs: Obecné neasociativní okruhy zkoumají okruhy a algebry bez axiomu asociativity v plné obecnosti a rozvíjejí strukturální pojmový aparát — ideály, radikály, nilpotenci, mocninnou asociativitu, alternativnost — který se specializuje na Lieovu, Jordanovu a další pojmenované podteorie. Poskytují jednotící rámec identit, variet algeber a univerzálních obalových konstrukcí, vůči němuž se poměřují konkrétnější neasociativní třídy, přičemž klasickým příkladem jsou alternativní algebry, jako jsou oktoniony.
nodes.brief:
Studies rings and algebras without the associativity axiom in full generality, developing the structural vocabulary — ideals, radicals, nilpotency, power-associativity, alternativity — that specializes into the Lie, Jordan, and other named subtheories. It provides the unifying framework (identities, varieties of algebras, universal enveloping constructions) against which more specific nonassociative classes are measured. Classical examples include alternative algebras (octoni

### Concepts
- **General nonassociative rings** (17Axx) — Studies rings and algebras that need not satisfy the associative law, including their basic structure theory, radicals, and identity-defined varieties such as alternative and flexible algebras.

nodes.name: Lie Algebras and Lie Superalgebras
nodes.en: Lie Algebras and Lie Superalgebras studies algebras defined by antisymmetry and the Jacobi identity, together with their Z2-graded super generalizations, forming one of the most extensively developed branches of algebra through its deep ties to Lie groups, differential geometry, and mathematical physics. It encompasses the classification of simple and semisimple Lie algebras, root systems, representation theory, Kac-Moody and affine Lie algebras, and the Lie superalgebras underlying supersymmetry, with the finite-dimensional semisimple case fully classified and infinite-dimensional and modular variants remaining active.
nodes.cs: Lieovy algebry a Lieovy superalgebry zkoumají algebry definované antisymetrií a Jacobiho identitou spolu s jejich Z2-odstupňovanými (super) zobecněními a tvoří jednu z nejrozvinutěji propracovaných větví algebry díky hlubokým vazbám na Lieovy grupy, diferenciální geometrii a matematickou fyziku. Zahrnují klasifikaci jednoduchých a polojednoduchých Lieových algeber, kořenové systémy, teorii reprezentací, Kac-Moodyho a afinní Lieovy algebry a Lieovy superalgebry, na nichž je založena supersymetrie, přičemž konečněrozměrný polojednoduchý případ je plně klasifikován a nekonečněrozměrné a modulární varianty zůstávají aktivním výzkumem.
nodes.brief:
Covers algebras defined by antisymmetry and the Jacobi identity, together with their Z2-graded (super) generalizations, forming one of the most extensively developed branches of algebra due to deep ties with Lie groups, differential geometry, and mathematical physics. Encompasses classification of simple and semisimple Lie algebras, root systems, representation theory, Kac-Moody and affine Lie algebras, and Lie superalgebras underlying supersymmetry. The finite-dimensional se

### Concepts
- **Lie algebras and Lie superalgebras** (17Bxx) — Studies algebras satisfying antisymmetry and the Jacobi identity and their Z2-graded superalgebra analogues, including their classification, structure, and representation theory, with the corresponding Lie groups treated under 22Exx.

nodes.name: Jordan Algebras, Triples, and Pairs
nodes.en: Jordan Algebras, Triples, and Pairs studies commutative nonassociative algebras satisfying the Jordan identity, originally introduced to axiomatize the algebra of quantum-mechanical observables, along with their generalizations to Jordan triple systems and Jordan pairs used in the theory of symmetric spaces and bounded symmetric domains. The classification of simple Jordan algebras parallels and interlocks with the classification of simple Lie algebras via the Tits-Kantor-Koecher construction, with the finite-dimensional case fully settled and infinite-dimensional variants an active area.
nodes.cs: Jordanovy algebry, trojice a páry zkoumají komutativní neasociativní algebry splňující Jordanovu identitu, původně zavedenou k axiomatizaci algebry kvantově-mechanických pozorovatelných veličin, spolu s jejich zobecněními na Jordanovy trojicové systémy a Jordanovy páry používané v teorii symetrických prostorů a ohraničených symetrických oblastí. Klasifikace jednoduchých Jordanových algeber se prolíná s klasifikací jednoduchých Lieových algeber prostřednictvím Titsovy-Kantorovy-Koecherovy konstrukce, přičemž konečněrozměrný případ je plně vyřešen a nekonečněrozměrné varianty zůstávají aktivním výzkumem.
nodes.brief:
Studies commutative nonassociative algebras satisfying the Jordan identity, originally introduced to axiomatize the algebra of observables in quantum mechanics, along with their generalizations to Jordan triple systems and Jordan pairs used in the theory of symmetric spaces and bounded symmetric domains. Classification of simple Jordan algebras parallels and interlocks with the classification of simple Lie algebras via the Tits-Kantor-Koecher construction. The finite-dimensio

### Concepts
- **Jordan algebras (algebras, triples and pairs)** (17Cxx) — Studies commutative algebras satisfying the Jordan identity together with their triple-system and pair generalizations, connecting to symmetric spaces and quantum-mechanical observable algebras.

nodes.name: Other Nonassociative Rings and Algebras
nodes.en: A residual class gathering nonassociative algebraic structures that fall outside the Lie and Jordan paradigms, such as Malcev algebras, quasigroup and loop-related algebras, Leibniz algebras, and other identity-defined nonassociative systems. These structures often arise as deformations or generalizations of Lie/Jordan theory and are studied both for their own structure theory and for applications in geometry and combinatorics. The area is heterogeneous, with pockets of maturity around Malcev and Leibniz algebras alongside many loosely connected identity-defined variants still under active classification.
nodes.cs: Zbytková třída neasociativních algebraických struktur, které nespadají do paradigmatu Lieových ani Jordanových algeber, jako jsou Malcevovy algebry, kvazigrupové a smyčkové struktury, Leibnizovy algebry a další systémy definované identitami. Tyto struktury často vznikají jako deformace nebo zobecnění Lieovy či Jordanovy teorie a zkoumají se jak pro vlastní strukturní teorii, tak pro aplikace v geometrii a kombinatorice. Obor je nesourodý, se zralými ostrůvky kolem Malcevových a Leibnizových algeber vedle mnoha volně propojených variant definovaných identitami, které se dosud aktivně klasifikují.
nodes.brief:
A residual class gathering nonassociative algebraic structures that fall outside the Lie and Jordan paradigms, such as Malcev algebras, quasigroup and loop-related algebras, Leibniz algebras, and other identity-defined nonassociative systems. These structures often arise as deformations or generalizations of Lie/Jordan theory and are studied both for their own structure theory and for applications in geometry and combinatorics. The area is heterogeneous, with pockets of matur

### Concepts
- **Other nonassociative rings and algebras** (17Dxx) — Covers nonassociative algebraic structures outside the Lie and Jordan families, such as Malcev and Leibniz algebras, unified mainly by not satisfying the standard associativity, antisymmetry-Jacobi, or Jordan identities.

nodes.name: Category theory; homological algebra
nodes.en: Category theory studies mathematical structures through objects, morphisms, and the functors and natural transformations relating them, providing a unifying language across algebra, geometry, and logic. Homological algebra applies this categorical framework to additive and abelian categories, developing derived and triangulated categories, derived functors, and spectral sequences as tools for measuring obstructions and computing invariants. Together they underpin higher-categorical and monoidal structures used throughout modern algebraic geometry and topology.
nodes.cs: Teorie kategorií zkoumá matematické struktury prostřednictvím objektů, morfismů a funktorů a přirozených transformací, které je propojují, a poskytuje tak jednotný jazyk pro algebru, geometrii a logiku. Homologická algebra aplikuje tento kategoriální rámec na aditivní a abelovské kategorie a rozvíjí odvozené a triangulované kategorie, odvozené funktory a spektrální posloupnosti jako nástroje k měření překážek a výpočtu invariantů. Společně tvoří základ vyšších kategoriálních a monoidálních struktur používaných v moderní algebraické geometrii a topologii.
nodes.brief:
Category theory is the grammar of modern mathematics: objects, morphisms, functors and natural transformations, with adjunctions and limits as its organizing devices. Homological algebra applies that grammar to abelian and triangulated categories, extracting derived functors, spectral sequences, and invariants that measure obstructions.

Both grew out of the algebraic topology of [[02.01.04.41]] and now serve as shared infrastructure: derived categories organize the sheaf cohomology of [[02.01.04.10]], resolutions compute the Ext and Tor of [[02.01.04.09]] and [[02.01.04.12]], and the K-theory of [[02.01.04.15]] is built categorically from the ground up. As a foundational alternative it converses with [[02.01.04.03]]; as an abstraction of algebraic structure, with [[02.01.04.06]].

nodes.name: Foundations of Categories and Functors
nodes.en: Establishes the basic language of category theory: objects, morphisms, functors, and natural transformations, together with distinguished classes of categories (toposes, groupoids, fibered categories) and the categorical presentation of algebraic and logical theories via sketches, Lawvere theories, and monads. This block forms the settled, axiomatic bedrock on which all other categorical machinery is built.
nodes.cs: Zakládá základní jazyk teorie kategorií: objekty, morfismy, funktory a přirozené transformace, spolu s vyznačenými třídami kategorií (topoi, grupoidy, fibrované kategorie) a kategoriální prezentací algebraických a logických teorií pomocí náčrtů (sketches), Lawvereho teorií a monád. Tento blok tvoří ustálený, axiomatický základ, na němž je vybudován veškerý další kategoriální aparát.
nodes.brief:
Establishes the basic language of category theory: objects, morphisms, functors, and natural transformations, together with distinguished classes of categories (toposes, groupoids, fibered categories) and the categorical presentation of algebraic and logical theories via sketches, Lawvere theories, and monads. This block forms the settled, axiomatic bedrock on which all other categorical machinery is built.

### Concepts
- **General theory of categories and functors** (18Axx) — Develops the basic language of objects, morphisms, functors, and natural transformations underlying all of category theory.
- **Special categories** (18Bxx) — Studies distinguished classes of categories, such as toposes, groupoids, ordered categories, and categories of relations, singled out by extra structure or properties.
- **Categories and theories** (18Cxx) — Studies categorical semantics of algebraic and logical theories through sketches, Lawvere theories, monads, and other presentations of theories as categorical data.

nodes.name: Categorical Structures and Homological Algebra
nodes.en: Covers enriched, internal, and higher-dimensional categorical structures such as bicategories, fibrations, and double categories, alongside the additive and abelian category axiomatics that generalize module and sheaf categories. Builds on these to develop derived categories, triangulated categories, derived functors, and spectral sequences, recasting classical homological algebra in fully categorical terms.
nodes.cs: Zahrnuje obohacené (enriched), vnitřní a vyšší-dimenzionální kategoriální struktury, jako jsou bikategorie, fibrace a dvojité kategorie, spolu s aditivní a abelovskou kategoriální axiomatikou, která zobecňuje kategorie modulů a svazků (sheaves). Na tomto základě rozvíjí odvozené kategorie, triangulované kategorie, odvozené funktory a spektrální posloupnosti a přetváří tak klasickou homologickou algebru zcela v kategoriálních pojmech.
nodes.brief:
Covers enriched, internal, and higher-dimensional categorical structures such as bicategories, fibrations, and double categories, alongside the additive and abelian category axiomatics that generalize module and sheaf categories. Builds on these to develop derived categories, triangulated categories, derived functors, and spectral sequences, recasting classical homological algebra in fully categorical terms.

### Concepts
- **Categorical structures** (18Dxx) — Studies enriched, internal, and higher-dimensional categorical structures, including bicategories, double categories, and fibered categories.
- **Categorical algebra** (18Exx) — Studies abelian, additive, and exact categories as the axiomatic framework generalizing categories of modules and sheaves.
- **Homological algebra in category theory, derived categories and functors** (18Gxx) — Develops derived categories, derived functors, triangulated categories, and spectral sequences as homological algebra formulated categorically.

nodes.name: Monoidal, Higher, and Geometric Categories
nodes.en: Studies categories with a tensor product — monoidal and braided monoidal categories, and the operads governing algebraic operations up to homotopy — together with infinity-categories and other higher-categorical, homotopical structures. Also covers the categorical frameworks, sheaves, stacks, and topos-theoretic geometry, that organize geometry and topology. These areas underpin derived algebraic geometry, topological field theory, and modern homotopy theory.
nodes.cs: Studuje kategorie s tenzorovým součinem — monoidální a splétané (braided) monoidální kategorie a operády řídící algebraické operace až na homotopii — spolu s nekonečno-kategoriemi (∞-kategoriemi) a dalšími vyššími kategoriálními a homotopickými strukturami. Zahrnuje také kategoriální rámce, svazky (sheaves), stacky a toposovou geometrii, které organizují geometrii a topologii. Tyto oblasti tvoří základ odvozené algebraické geometrie, topologické teorie pole a moderní teorie homotopie.
nodes.brief:
Studies categories with a tensor product — monoidal and braided monoidal categories, and the operads governing algebraic operations up to homotopy — together with infinity-categories and other higher-categorical, homotopical structures. Also covers the categorical frameworks, sheaves, stacks, and topos-theoretic geometry, that organize geometry and topology. These areas underpin derived algebraic geometry, topological field theory, and modern homotopy theory.

### Concepts
- **Categories in geometry and topology** (18Fxx) — Studies sheaves, stacks, and topos-theoretic categorical frameworks used to organize geometric and topological structures.
- **Monoidal categories and operads** (18Mxx) — Studies categories equipped with a tensor product and the operads that encode algebraic operations and their coherence up to homotopy.
- **Higher categories and homotopical algebra** (18Nxx) — Studies infinity-categories and other higher-categorical structures together with the homotopical algebra used to model them, a major research frontier.

nodes.name: K-theory
nodes.en: K-theory assigns algebraic invariants — the K-groups — to rings, categories, topological spaces, and operator algebras by classifying their projective modules or vector bundles up to stable equivalence. It spans classical low-degree K-groups, Quillen's higher algebraic K-theory with its geometric and arithmetic applications, topological K-theory as an extraordinary cohomology theory, and the K-theory of operator algebras used in index theory and the Baum-Connes and Novikov conjectures.
nodes.cs: K-teorie přiřazuje okruhům, kategoriím, topologickým prostorům a operátorovým algebrám algebraické invarianty — K-grupy — klasifikací jejich projektivních modulů nebo vektorových svazků až na stabilní ekvivalenci. Zahrnuje klasické nízkostupňové K-grupy, Quillenovu vyšší algebraickou K-teorii s jejími geometrickými a aritmetickými aplikacemi, topologickou K-teorii jako mimořádnou kohomologickou teorii a K-teorii operátorových algeber používanou v teorii indexu a v Baumově–Connesově a Novikovově hypotéze.
nodes.brief:
K-theory is a machine for turning geometry and algebra into stable invariants: projective modules over a ring, vector bundles over a space, classified up to stable equivalence and organized into K-groups. Quillen's higher algebraic K-theory, topological K-theory as an extraordinary cohomology theory, and the K-theory of operator algebras are its three main incarnations.

It is a born interdisciplinarian: constructed with the categorical machinery of [[02.01.04.14]], computing invariants for the varieties of [[02.01.04.10]] and the spaces of [[02.01.04.41]], and — through the C*-algebraic K-theory rooted in [[02.01.04.34]] — driving the index theory on manifolds of [[02.01.04.43]] via the Baum-Connes and Novikov programs.

nodes.name: Classical K-Groups: K₀, K₁, K₂
nodes.en: The foundational low-degree algebraic K-groups of rings and categories: K₀ classifies stable isomorphism classes of projective modules via the Grothendieck construction, K₁ captures automorphisms modulo elementary matrices through Whitehead groups, and K₂ measures relations among elementary matrices via Steinberg groups and symbols. These groups underlie classical applications such as ideal class groups, Whitehead torsion in topology, and Matsumoto's description of K₂ of fields in terms of Steinberg symbols.
nodes.cs: Základní nízkostupňové algebraické K-grupy okruhů a kategorií: K₀ klasifikuje třídy stabilní izomorfie projektivních modulů pomocí Grothendieckovy konstrukce, K₁ zachycuje automorfismy modulo elementární matice prostřednictvím Whiteheadových grup a K₂ měří vztahy mezi elementárními maticemi pomocí Steinbergových grup a symbolů. Tyto grupy jsou základem klasických aplikací, jako jsou grupy ideálních tříd, Whiteheadova torze v topologii a Matsumotův popis K₂ těles pomocí Steinbergových symbolů.
nodes.brief:
The foundational low-degree algebraic K-groups of rings and categories: K₀ classifies stable isomorphism classes of projective modules via the Grothendieck construction, K₁ captures automorphisms modulo elementary matrices through Whitehead groups, and K₂ measures relations among elementary matrices via Steinberg groups and symbols. These groups underlie classical applications such as ideal class groups, Whitehead torsion in topology, and Matsumoto's description of K₂ of fiel

### Concepts
- **Grothendieck groups and K_0** (19Axx) — Studies K_0(R), the group completion of the monoid of stably free or projective modules over a ring, category, or space, classifying vector bundles and projective modules up to stable isomorphism.
- **Whitehead groups and K_1** (19Bxx) — Studies K_1(R), generated by automorphisms of free modules modulo elementary-matrix equivalence, encoding units of rings and the Whitehead torsion invariant of homotopy equivalences.
- **Steinberg groups and K_2** (19Cxx) — Studies K_2(R) via the Steinberg group presentation of relations among elementary matrices, tied to universal central extensions of linear groups and Hilbert symbols in local and global fields.

nodes.name: Higher, Geometric, and Arithmetic K-Theory
nodes.en: Quillen's higher K-groups extend K_0, K_1, K_2 via the plus-construction or Q-construction into a full sequence of functors bridging algebra, geometry, and number theory. This block covers higher algebraic K-theory itself alongside its deployment in geometric settings (bundles, Chow groups, motivic cohomology), arithmetic settings (special values of zeta and L-functions, étale cohomology), and the Hermitian K-theory of quadratic and bilinear forms.
nodes.cs: Quillenovy vyšší K-grupy rozšiřují K₀, K₁, K₂ pomocí plus-konstrukce nebo Q-konstrukce do úplné posloupnosti funktorů propojujících algebru, geometrii a teorii čísel. Tento blok zahrnuje samotnou vyšší algebraickou K-teorii i její uplatnění v geometrických kontextech (svazky, Chowovy grupy, motivická kohomologie), aritmetických kontextech (speciální hodnoty zeta- a L-funkcí, étalová kohomologie) a Hermitovskou K-teorii kvadratických a bilineárních forem.
nodes.brief:
Quillen's higher K-groups extend K_0, K_1, K_2 via the plus-construction or Q-construction into a full sequence of functors bridging algebra, geometry, and number theory. This block covers higher algebraic K-theory itself alongside its deployment in geometric settings (bundles, Chow groups, motivic cohomology), arithmetic settings (special values of zeta and L-functions, étale cohomology), and the Hermitian K-theory of quadratic and bilinear forms.

### Concepts
- **Higher algebraic K-theory** (19Dxx) — Constructs and studies the higher K-groups K_n(R) for n greater than or equal to 2 via Quillen's plus-construction or Q-construction applied to categories of modules or vector bundles.
- **K-theory in geometry** (19Exx) — Applies algebraic K-theory to geometric objects such as schemes and vector bundles, connecting K-groups to Chow groups, algebraic cycles, and motivic cohomology.
- **K-theory in number theory** (19Fxx) — Relates K-groups of rings of integers and number fields to arithmetic invariants including class numbers, special zeta and L-function values, and étale cohomology, as in the Quillen-Lichtenbaum and Bloch-Kato conjectures.
- **K-theory of forms** (19Gxx) — Studies Hermitian and quadratic K-theory, the K-theoretic classification of symmetric, skew-symmetric, and quadratic bilinear forms over rings equipped with an involution.

nodes.name: Topological K-Theory and Its Obstructions
nodes.en: Topological K-theory assigns to a space the Grothendieck group of complex or real vector bundles over it, forming an extraordinary cohomology theory governed by Bott periodicity and central to index theory and stable homotopy computations. Closely related obstruction-theoretic invariants in low-degree K-groups detect failures of finiteness or simple-homotopy equivalence in manifold and CW-complex topology.
nodes.cs: Topologická K-teorie přiřazuje prostoru Grothendieckovu grupu komplexních nebo reálných vektorových svazků nad ním a tvoří tak mimořádnou kohomologickou teorii řízenou Bottovou periodicitou, klíčovou pro teorii indexu a výpočty ve stabilní homotopii. Úzce související obstrukčně-teoretické invarianty v nízkostupňových K-grupách odhalují selhání konečnosti nebo jednoduché homotopické ekvivalence v topologii variet a CW-komplexů.
nodes.brief:
Topological K-theory assigns to a space the Grothendieck group of complex or real vector bundles over it, forming an extraordinary cohomology theory governed by Bott periodicity and central to index theory and stable homotopy computations. Closely related obstruction-theoretic invariants in low-degree K-groups detect failures of finiteness or simple-homotopy equivalence in manifold and CW-complex topology.

### Concepts
- **Obstructions from topology** (19Jxx) — Studies K-theoretic obstructions to geometric problems, notably the Wall finiteness obstruction and Whitehead torsion, which control finiteness and simple-homotopy equivalence of spaces.
- **Topological K-theory** (19Lxx) — Studies the K^0 and K^1 functors of complex or real vector bundles over topological spaces, Bott periodicity, and the resulting generalized cohomology theory used throughout index theory.

nodes.name: K-Theory of Operator Algebras and Applications
nodes.en: K-theory of C*-algebras and von Neumann algebras, including bivariant KK-theory, classifies operator algebras and drives the Baum-Connes and Novikov conjectures linking functional analysis, geometry, and topology. This block also gathers miscellaneous further applications of K-theoretic methods that fall outside the primary algebraic, geometric, arithmetic, and topological classifications.
nodes.cs: K-teorie C*-algeber a von Neumannových algeber, včetně bivariantní KK-teorie, klasifikuje operátorové algebry a je hnací silou Baumovy–Connesovy a Novikovovy hypotézy propojující funkcionální analýzu, geometrii a topologii. Tento blok rovněž shromažďuje různé další aplikace K-teoretických metod, které nespadají do hlavních algebraických, geometrických, aritmetických a topologických klasifikací.
nodes.brief:
K-theory of C*-algebras and von Neumann algebras, including bivariant KK-theory, classifies operator algebras and drives the Baum-Connes and Novikov conjectures linking functional analysis, geometry, and topology. This block also gathers miscellaneous further applications of K-theoretic methods that fall outside the primary algebraic, geometric, arithmetic, and topological classifications.

### Concepts
- **K-theory and operator algebras** (19Kxx) — Studies K-groups and Kasparov's bivariant KK-theory of C*-algebras and von Neumann algebras, foundational to noncommutative geometry and operator-algebraic index theory.
- **Miscellaneous applications of K-theory** (19Mxx) — Covers applications of K-theoretic methods to problems that do not fall under the primary algebraic, geometric, arithmetic, or operator-algebraic classifications above.

nodes.name: Group theory and generalizations
nodes.en: Group theory studies sets equipped with an associative, invertible binary operation, developing the structure and classification theory of finite and infinite groups alongside their actions, representations, and linear algebraic realizations. It extends to the commutative case of abelian groups and their cohomological and categorical generalizations such as groupoids, and further generalizes the group concept to semigroups, quasigroups, and loops, including probabilistic methods such as random walks on groups.
nodes.cs: Teorie grup zkoumá množiny vybavené asociativní, invertibilní binární operací a rozvíjí strukturní a klasifikační teorii konečných i nekonečných grup spolu s jejich akcemi, reprezentacemi a lineárními algebraickými realizacemi. Rozšiřuje se na komutativní případ abelovských grup a jejich kohomologická a kategoriální zobecnění, jako jsou grupoidy, a dále zobecňuje pojem grupy na pologrupy, kvazigrupy a smyčky, včetně pravděpodobnostních metod, jako jsou náhodné procházky na grupách.
nodes.brief:
Groups formalize symmetry: one associative, invertible operation, from finite simple groups — whose classification is among the largest theorems ever assembled — to infinite, geometric and combinatorial groups presented by generators and relations. Representations, group actions and cohomology are its levers; abelian groups, groupoids, semigroups, quasigroups and loops its tamer and wilder generalizations.

Galois groups tie the pillar to [[02.01.04.08]], fundamental groups to the topology of [[02.01.04.41]] and [[02.01.04.42]], random walks on groups to [[02.01.04.44]], and continuous symmetry — Lie groups — is split off into [[02.01.04.17]]. As the mathematics of symmetry in physical systems it is standing infrastructure for [[01.01]].

nodes.name: Foundations and Structural Classification
nodes.en: Establishes the axiomatic language of group theory and develops the structure and classification theory of both finite and infinite groups. Includes the machinery of subgroup lattices, composition series, and extension theory that underlies the largely completed classification of finite simple groups, alongside special structural properties such as nilpotency, solvability, growth, and geometric group theory that remain actively studied for infinite groups. Serves as the conceptual foundation on which the group-theoretic branches covered by the sibling blocks — representation theory, abelian and categorical extensions, and semigroup generalizations — are built.
nodes.cs: Zakládá axiomatický jazyk teorie grup a rozvíjí strukturní a klasifikační teorii konečných i nekonečných grup. Zahrnuje aparát podgrupových svazů, kompozičních řad a teorie rozšíření, na němž stojí z velké části dokončená klasifikace konečných jednoduchých grup, spolu se speciálními strukturními vlastnostmi, jako jsou nilpotence, řešitelnost, růst a geometrická teorie grup, které zůstávají u nekonečných grup předmětem aktivního výzkumu.
