It lives inside the spaces of [[02.01.04.34]] and acts on the equations of [[02.01.04.24]], [[02.01.04.25]] and [[02.01.04.33]] — semigroup theory is abstract evolution, Sturm-Liouville theory is spectral ODE analysis. Self-adjoint operators are the observables of quantum mechanics, making the pillar standing infrastructure for [[01.01]]; index theory connects it to [[02.01.04.15]] and [[02.01.04.43]].

nodes.name: Foundations and Algebraic Structures of Linear Operators
nodes.en: Covers the general axiomatic theory of bounded and unbounded linear operators on Banach and Hilbert spaces, the classification of distinguished special classes (self-adjoint, normal, compact, Fredholm, subnormal), the study of individual operators as elements of rings and algebras, and the linear spaces and algebras of operators (operator ideals, C*- and von Neumann-algebra-adjacent structures) they generate. This is the settled, textbook core of operator theory, from which the differential, integral and nonlinear branches diverge.
nodes.cs: Zahrnuje obecnou axiomatickou teorii omezených i neomezených lineárních operátorů na Banachových a Hilbertových prostorech, klasifikaci vyznačených tříd (samoadjungované, normální, kompaktní, Fredholmovy, subnormální operátory), studium jednotlivých operátorů jako prvků okruhů a algeber a lineární prostory a algebry operátorů (operátorové ideály, struktury blízké C*- a von Neumannovým algebrám), které generují. Jde o ustálené, učebnicové jádro teorie operátorů, z něhož se odvíjejí diferenciální, integrální a nelineární větve.
nodes.brief:
Covers the general axiomatic theory of bounded and unbounded linear operators on Banach and Hilbert spaces, the classification of distinguished special classes (self-adjoint, normal, compact, Fredholm, subnormal), the study of individual operators as elements of rings, algebras, and other algebraic systems, and the linear spaces and algebras (operator ideals, C*- and von Neumann-algebra-adjacent structures) they generate. This is the settled, textbook core of operator theory 

### Concepts
- **General theory of linear operators** (47Axx) — Develops the foundational spectral, functional-calculus, and structural theory of bounded and unbounded linear operators on Banach and Hilbert spaces.
- **Special classes of linear operators** (47Bxx) — Classifies distinguished operator families — self-adjoint, normal, compact, Fredholm, Toeplitz, composition, and related classes — by their defining structural and spectral properties.
- **Individual linear operators as elements of algebraic systems** (47Cxx) — Studies single operators viewed as elements of rings, algebras, or other algebraic structures, emphasizing their algebraic rather than purely analytic behavior.
- **Linear spaces and algebras of operators** (47Lxx) — Investigates operator ideals, operator algebras, and other linear spaces of operators as structured objects in their own right, including their norms and representations.

nodes.name: Differential, Integral, and Semigroup Operators
nodes.en: Groups the operator-theoretic study of ordinary and partial differential operators, integral and pseudodifferential operators, and the one-parameter groups and semigroups of linear operators that model their associated evolution equations. This area is the analytic bridge between abstract operator theory and PDE, harmonic analysis, and mathematical physics, underpinning existence, regularity, and long-time-behavior results for evolution processes.
nodes.cs: Sdružuje operátorové studium obyčejných a parciálních diferenciálních operátorů, integrálních a pseudodiferenciálních operátorů a jednoparametrových grup a pologrup lineárních operátorů, které modelují jimi generované evoluční rovnice. Tato oblast je analytickým mostem mezi abstraktní teorií operátorů a parciálními diferenciálními rovnicemi, harmonickou analýzou a matematickou fyzikou a je základem výsledků o existenci, regularitě a dlouhodobém chování evolučních procesů.
nodes.brief:
Groups the operator-theoretic study of ordinary and partial differential operators, integral and pseudodifferential operators, and the one-parameter groups and semigroups of linear operators that model their associated evolution equations. This area is the analytic bridge between abstract operator theory and PDE, harmonic analysis, and mathematical physics, underpinning existence, regularity, and long-time-behavior results for evolution processes. Semigroup generation theorem

### Concepts
- **Groups and semigroups of linear operators, their generalizations and applications** (47Dxx) — Studies one-parameter (and generalized) groups and semigroups of operators, notably C0-semigroups, and their use in solving abstract evolution equations.
- **Ordinary differential operators** (47Exx) — Analyzes ordinary differential operators as linear operators, including their spectral theory, self-adjoint extensions, and boundary-value structure.
- **Partial differential operators** (47Fxx) — Treats partial differential operators through the lens of operator theory, addressing their domains, spectra, and well-posedness as unbounded operators.
- **Integral, integro-differential, and pseudodifferential operators** (47Gxx) — Covers integral, integro-differential, and pseudodifferential operators, including their symbol calculus, boundedness, and compactness properties.

nodes.name: Nonlinear Operator Theory, Equations, and Applications
nodes.en: Encompasses nonlinear operators and their intrinsic properties, the equations and inequalities they generate (variational inequalities, fixed-point problems), the diverse applications of operator theory across analysis and mathematical physics, and nonclassical operator-theoretic frameworks that extend beyond the linear, classical setting. This cluster sits at the research frontier where monotonicity methods, fixed-point theory, and generalized nonclassical operator structures converge.
nodes.cs: Zahrnuje nelineární operátory a jejich vlastní vlastnosti, rovnice a nerovnice, které generují (variační nerovnice, úlohy o pevném bodě), rozmanité aplikace teorie operátorů napříč analýzou a matematickou fyzikou a neklasické operátorové rámce přesahující lineární klasické nastavení. Tento shluk leží na výzkumné hranici, kde se setkávají metody monotonie, teorie pevného bodu a zobecněné neklasické operátorové struktury.
nodes.brief:
Encompasses nonlinear operators and their intrinsic properties, the equations and inequalities they generate (variational inequalities, fixed-point problems), the diverse applications of operator theory across analysis and mathematical physics, and nonclassical operator-theoretic frameworks that extend beyond the linear, classical setting. This cluster sits at the research frontier where monotonicity methods, fixed-point theory, and generalized operator structures (e.g., non-

### Concepts
- **Nonlinear operators and their properties** (47Hxx) — Studies nonlinear operators on normed or topological spaces, including monotonicity, accretivity, continuity, and fixed-point properties.
- **Equations and inequalities involving nonlinear operators** (47Jxx) — Analyzes equations and variational inequalities defined by nonlinear operators, including existence, uniqueness, and iterative solution methods.
- **Miscellaneous applications of operator theory** (47Nxx) — Collects applications of operator-theoretic methods to problems in other areas of analysis, mathematical physics, and applied mathematics.
- **Other (nonclassical) types of operator theory** (47Sxx) — Covers nonclassical operator-theoretic frameworks that fall outside the standard linear or nonlinear taxonomy, including emerging generalized settings.

nodes.name: Calculus of variations and optimal control; optimization
nodes.en: Calculus of variations and optimal control studies the existence and characterization of minimizers of functionals and optimal trajectories of dynamical systems, through Euler-Lagrange and Pontryagin optimality conditions, Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman value-function theory, and numerical optimization methods, together with the geometric and physical variational methods — minimal surfaces, spectral variational principles, least action — that connect these techniques to differential geometry and physics.
nodes.cs: Variační počet a optimální řízení zkoumají existenci a charakterizaci minimizérů funkcionálů a optimálních trajektorií dynamických systémů pomocí Eulerových-Lagrangeových a Pontrjaginových podmínek optimality, teorie hodnotové funkce Hamiltonovy-Jacobiho-Bellmanovy rovnice a numerických optimalizačních metod, spolu s geometrickými a fyzikálními variačními metodami — minimálními plochami, spektrálními variačními principy, principem nejmenší akce — jež tyto techniky propojují s diferenciální geometrií a fyzikou.
nodes.brief:
From the brachistochrone to modern geometric analysis, this pillar asks which curve, surface or control steers a functional to its extremum: Euler-Lagrange equations, Pontryagin's maximum principle, Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman theory, and the direct methods that trade smoothness for compactness. Least-action principles make it the variational face of [[01.01]].

Its optimality conditions are differential equations analyzed with the tools of [[02.01.04.25]] and [[02.01.04.24]]; minimal surfaces and geometric variational problems face [[02.01.04.39]] and [[02.01.04.43]]; finite-dimensional and algorithmic optimization is the neighboring [[02.01.04.48]]; and feedback-oriented control engineering is split off into [[02.01.04.50]].

nodes.name: Foundations of Variational and Optimal Control Theory
nodes.en: Covers the core analytic machinery of the calculus of variations and optimal control: proving that minimizers or optimal trajectories exist, characterizing them via first- and second-order optimality conditions (Euler-Lagrange, Pontryagin maximum principle, transversality), propagating value functions through Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman theory, and computing solutions numerically when closed forms are unavailable. This cluster is the backbone that every applied variational or control problem ultimately rests on.
nodes.cs: Zahrnuje jádro analytického aparátu variačního počtu a optimálního řízení: důkaz existence minimizérů či optimálních trajektorií, jejich charakterizaci pomocí podmínek optimality prvního a druhého řádu (Eulerovy-Lagrangeovy rovnice, Pontrjaginův princip maxima, transverzalita), šíření hodnotové funkce prostřednictvím Hamiltonovy-Jacobiho-Bellmanovy teorie a numerický výpočet řešení tam, kde uzavřený tvar neexistuje. Tento shluk tvoří páteř, na níž nakonec stojí každá aplikovaná variační nebo řídicí úloha.
nodes.brief:
Covers the core analytic machinery of the calculus of variations and optimal control: proving that minimizers or optimal trajectories exist, characterizing them via first- and second-order optimality conditions (Euler-Lagrange, Pontryagin maximum principle, transversality), propagating value functions through Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman theory, and computing solutions numerically when closed forms are unavailable. This cluster is the backbone that every applied variational proble

### Concepts
- **Existence theories in calculus of variations and optimal control** (49Jxx) — Studies conditions — coercivity, lower semicontinuity, compactness, convexity — under which minimizers of variational functionals or optimal controls actually exist.
- **Optimality conditions** (49Kxx) — Derives necessary and sufficient first- and second-order conditions, such as the Euler-Lagrange equations and the Pontryagin maximum principle, that characterize minimizers and optimal trajectories.
- **Hamilton-Jacobi theories** (49Lxx) — Develops the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman approach to optimal control and the calculus of variations, including viscosity-solution theory for the associated first-order PDEs.
- **Numerical methods in optimal control** (49Mxx) — Constructs and analyzes discretization, direct/indirect, and dynamic-programming algorithms for computing approximate solutions to optimal control and variational problems.
- **Miscellaneous topics in calculus of variations and optimal control** (49Nxx) — Collects auxiliary and cross-cutting topics in calculus of variations and optimal control not captured by the other 49-XX subclasses, such as duality, sensitivity, and inverse problems.

nodes.name: Geometric and Physical Variational Methods
nodes.en: Concerns variational structures tied to geometry, spectral theory, and physics: minimization problems posed on manifolds or via geometric measure theory (minimal surfaces, currents, sets of finite perimeter), variational characterizations of eigenvalues of differential and integral operators, and the least-action and stationary-action principles underlying classical and continuum physics. These subclasses sit at the interface of calculus of variations with differential geometry, spectral analysis, and theoretical physics.
nodes.cs: Týká se variačních struktur spjatých s geometrií, spektrální teorií a fyzikou: minimalizačních úloh na varietách nebo pomocí geometrické teorie míry (minimální plochy, proudy, množiny konečného obvodu), variačních charakterizací vlastních čísel diferenciálních a integrálních operátorů a principů nejmenší a stacionární akce, na nichž stojí klasická a kontinuová fyzika. Tyto podtřídy leží na rozhraní variačního počtu s diferenciální geometrií, spektrální analýzou a teoretickou fyzikou.
nodes.brief:
Concerns variational structures tied to geometry, spectral theory, and physics: minimization problems posed on manifolds or via geometric measure theory (minimal surfaces, currents, sets of finite perimeter), variational characterizations of eigenvalues of differential and integral operators, and the least-action and stationary-action principles underlying classical and continuum physics. These subclasses sit at the interface of 49-XX with differential geometry, spectral anal

### Concepts
- **Manifolds and measure-geometric topics** (49Qxx) — Treats variational problems formulated on manifolds and via geometric measure theory, including minimal surfaces, currents, and shape optimization.
- **Variational methods for eigenvalues of operators** (49Rxx) — Uses Rayleigh quotients and min-max variational characterizations to study and estimate eigenvalues of differential and other operators.
- **Variational principles of physics** (49Sxx) — Formalizes least-action and other stationary-action principles that derive the equations of motion of physical systems from variational functionals.

nodes.name: Geometry
nodes.en: Geometry (MSC 51-XX) studies spatial structures through incidence, order, metric and coordinate methods distinct from the algebraic and differential-geometric branches classified elsewhere — spanning axiomatic incidence geometry in the Hilbertian tradition, ordered and closure structures generalizing betweenness and convexity, metric and topological geometry including distance-geometry reconstruction, and classical real, complex and analytic geometry linking synthetic reasoning to physics.
nodes.cs: Geometrie (MSC 51-XX) zkoumá prostorové struktury pomocí incidenčních, uspořádaných, metrických a souřadnicových metod, odlišných od algebraické a diferenciální geometrie klasifikovaných jinde — zahrnuje axiomatickou incidenční geometrii v hilbertovské tradici, uspořádané a uzávěrové struktury zobecňující meziležení a konvexitu, metrickou a topologickou geometrii včetně rekonstrukce z předepsaných vzdáleností a klasickou reálnou, komplexní a analytickou geometrii propojující syntetické uvažování s fyzikou.
nodes.brief:
This is geometry in the synthetic and foundational key: incidence structures and axiomatics in the Hilbert tradition, ordered and closure structures generalizing betweenness and convexity, metric and distance geometry, and classical real, complex and analytic geometry. Projective and affine planes, buildings, and finite geometries are characteristic objects.

Its axiomatic program is a standing application of the logic in [[02.01.04.03]]; finite and incidence geometries trade with the designs of [[02.01.04.04]]; convexity questions mature into [[02.01.04.38]]; smooth structures into the differential geometry of [[02.01.04.39]]; and the coordinate method hands algebraic questions to [[02.01.04.10]] and [[02.01.04.08]].

nodes.name: Incidence Geometry and Axiomatic Foundations
nodes.en: Studies geometric structures defined purely by incidence relations among points, lines, and higher flats, abstracted from classical affine and projective geometry through synthetic axiom systems in the tradition of Hilbert's Grundlagen. Encompasses generalizations to non-Desarguesian and non-Pappian planes, coordinatization by rings rather than fields (Hjelmslev and Barbilian planes), and finite combinatorial incidence structures such as generalized polygons, nets, and Steiner systems.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá geometrické struktury definované čistě incidenčními vztahy mezi body, přímkami a vyššími rovinami, abstrahované z klasické afinní a projektivní geometrie prostřednictvím syntetických axiomatických systémů v tradici Hilbertových Grundlagen. Zahrnuje zobecnění na nedesarguesovské a nepappovské roviny, souřadnicování okruhy namísto tělesy (Hjelmslevovy a Barbilianovy roviny) a konečné kombinatorické incidenční struktury, jako jsou zobecněné mnohoúhelníky, sítě a Steinerovy systémy.
nodes.brief:
Studies geometric structures defined purely by incidence relations among points, lines, and higher flats, abstracted from classical affine and projective geometry through synthetic axiom systems in the tradition of Hilbert's Grundlagen. Encompasses generalizations to non-Desarguesian and non-Pappian planes, coordinatization by rings rather than fields (Hjelmslev and Barbilian planes), and finite combinatorial incidence structures such as generalized polygons, nets, and Steine

### Concepts
- **Linear incidence geometry** (51Axx) — Studies affine and projective planes and spaces axiomatized by linear incidence and parallelism relations among points and lines.
- **Nonlinear incidence geometry** (51Bxx) — Extends incidence-geometric axiomatics to structures whose lines or blocks need not behave linearly, including generalized polygons and near-geometries.
- **Ring geometry (Hjelmslev, Barbilian, etc.)** (51Cxx) — Develops geometries coordinatized over rings rather than fields, such as Hjelmslev planes and Barbilian spaces, where incidence can fail uniqueness.
- **Finite geometry and special incidence structures** (51Exx) — Classifies finite projective and affine planes, generalized quadrangles, and combinatorial designs arising from finite incidence structures.
- **Incidence groups** (51Jxx) — Studies groups acting on incidence structures and the algebraic structures induced on point sets by such group actions.

nodes.name: Ordered and Closure Geometric Structures
nodes.en: Investigates incidence structures enriched with an order or betweenness relation, and abstract closure operators that capture spanning or convex-hull behavior independently of coordinates. Ordered geometries axiomatize the notion of betweenness underlying real affine geometry without invoking metric or field structure, while geometric closure systems formalize dependency via matroid-like axioms, and geometric order structures extend these convexity ideas toward links with differential-geometric causal structures.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá incidenční struktury obohacené o vztah uspořádání nebo meziležení a abstraktní uzávěrové operátory, které zachycují chování rozpětí či konvexního obalu nezávisle na souřadnicích. Uspořádané geometrie axiomatizují pojem meziležení, na němž stojí reálná afinní geometrie, bez odkazu na metrickou nebo tělesovou strukturu, zatímco geometrické uzávěrové systémy formalizují závislost pomocí matroidům podobných axiomů a geometrické uspořádané struktury tyto konvexní představy rozšiřují směrem k souvislostem s diferenciálně-geometrickými kauzálními strukturami.
nodes.brief:
Investigates incidence structures enriched with an order or betweenness relation, and abstract closure operators that capture spanning or convex-hull behavior independently of coordinates. Ordered geometries axiomatize the notion of 'betweenness' underlying real affine geometry without invoking metric or field structure, while geometric closure systems formalize dependency via matroid-like axioms. Geometric order structures extend these convexity ideas toward links with diffe

### Concepts
- **Geometric closure systems** (51Dxx) — Axiomatizes spanning and dependency in geometric structures via closure operators generalizing linear and matroid dependence.
- **Ordered geometries (ordered incidence structures, etc.)** (51Gxx) — Studies incidence structures equipped with a betweenness or order relation that axiomatizes affine order without reference to a metric.
- **Geometric order structures** (51Lxx) — Examines order structures on geometric spaces generalizing betweenness, with connections to causal and convexity structures in differential geometry.

nodes.name: Metric, Topological, and Distance Geometry
nodes.en: Studies geometric structures equipped with a distance function or a compatible topology, generalizing Euclidean and non-Euclidean metric spaces beyond a purely axiomatic incidence framework. Distance geometry addresses reconstructing point configurations from prescribed pairwise distances, a problem central to rigidity theory, molecular conformation, and sensor-network localization, while topological geometry studies incidence structures whose point and line sets carry compatible topologies.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá geometrické struktury vybavené funkcí vzdálenosti nebo kompatibilní topologií, zobecňující eukleidovské a neeukleidovské metrické prostory nad rámec čistě axiomatického incidenčního přístupu. Geometrie vzdáleností řeší rekonstrukci konfigurací bodů z předepsaných párových vzdáleností, což je úloha ústřední pro teorii tuhosti, molekulární konformaci a lokalizaci v senzorových sítích, zatímco topologická geometrie zkoumá incidenční struktury, jejichž množiny bodů a přímek nesou kompatibilní topologie.
nodes.brief:
Studies geometric structures equipped with a distance function or a compatible topology, generalizing Euclidean and non-Euclidean metric spaces beyond a purely axiomatic incidence framework. Distance geometry addresses reconstructing point configurations from prescribed pairwise distances, a problem central to rigidity theory, molecular conformation, and sensor-network localization. Topological geometry studies incidence structures whose point and line sets carry compatible t

### Concepts
- **Metric geometry** (51Fxx) — Studies geometries axiomatized by a distance function satisfying metric-space or generalized-metric axioms compatible with incidence.
- **Topological geometry** (51Hxx) — Studies incidence structures such as projective and affine planes whose point and line sets carry topologies compatible with incidence.
- **Distance geometry** (51Kxx) — Studies the reconstruction and rigidity of point configurations from prescribed pairwise distances.

nodes.name: Real, Complex, Analytic, and Applied Classical Geometry
nodes.en: Covers geometry over the real and complex fields treated by elementary and synthetic methods, including hyperbolic and elliptic non-Euclidean geometry, alongside coordinate-based analytic and descriptive geometry historically used for technical drawing and projection. Classical or axiomatic geometry and physics connects synthetic geometric reasoning to physical theories such as mechanics and relativity, requiring cross-classification with the relevant physics sections.
nodes.cs: Zahrnuje geometrii nad reálným a komplexním tělesem zkoumanou elementárními a syntetickými metodami, včetně hyperbolické a eliptické neeukleidovské geometrie, spolu se souřadnicovou analytickou a deskriptivní geometrií historicky užívanou pro technické kreslení a promítání. Klasická neboli axiomatická geometrie a fyzika propojuje syntetické geometrické uvažování s fyzikálními teoriemi, jako je mechanika a relativita, což vyžaduje souběžnou klasifikaci s příslušnými fyzikálními obory.
nodes.brief:
Covers geometry over the real and complex fields treated by elementary and synthetic methods, including hyperbolic and elliptic non-Euclidean geometry, alongside coordinate-based analytic and descriptive geometry historically used for technical drawing and projection. Classical or axiomatic geometry and physics connects synthetic geometric reasoning to physical theories such as mechanics and relativity, requiring cross-classification with the relevant physics sections. This b

### Concepts
- **Real and complex geometry** (51Mxx) — Treats elementary and synthetic geometry over the real and complex numbers, including hyperbolic, elliptic, and other non-Euclidean geometries.
- **Analytic and descriptive geometry** (51Nxx) — Studies geometry via coordinate methods and projective drawing techniques used for technical representation and descriptive construction.
- **Classical or axiomatic geometry and physics** (51Pxx) — Applies classical or axiomatic geometric methods to physical theories such as mechanics and relativity, requiring joint classification with physics sections.

nodes.name: Convex and discrete geometry
nodes.en: Convex and discrete geometry studies the metric, affine and combinatorial invariants of convex bodies and polytopes — volumes, support functions, face lattices — alongside the combinatorics of discrete configurations of points, lines and convex sets, covering incidences, packings, coverings, tilings, and extremal and Helly-type theorems that connect geometry to combinatorics and computational geometry.
nodes.cs: Konvexní a diskrétní geometrie zkoumá metrické, afinní a kombinatorické invarianty konvexních těles a mnohostěnů — objemy, opěrné funkce, svazy stěn — spolu s kombinatorikou diskrétních konfigurací bodů, přímek a konvexních množin, včetně incidencí, pakování, pokrytí, dláždění a extremálních vět a vět Hellyho typu, jež propojují geometrii s kombinatorikou a výpočetní geometrií.
nodes.brief:
Convex bodies and their metric, affine and combinatorial invariants — volumes, mixed volumes, support functions, face lattices — meet the discrete geometry of packings, coverings, tilings, incidences and Helly-type theorems. The Brunn-Minkowski inequality and the combinatorics of polytopes are its twin classical cores.

Polytopes are the feasible regions of the linear programming in [[02.01.04.48]]; incidence and extremal configuration problems are shared ground with [[02.01.04.04]]; algorithmic questions belong to the computational geometry of [[02.01.04.47]]; convexity in analysis returns in [[02.01.04.18]] and [[02.01.04.36]]; and the synthetic parent discipline is [[02.01.04.37]].

nodes.name: Convex Bodies and Polytopes
nodes.en: Studies the geometry of convex sets in real vector spaces — their metric, affine, and combinatorial invariants (volume, mixed volumes, support functions, Minkowski sums) — and specializes to polytopes and polyhedra, the finitely generated convex bodies whose face lattices, f-vectors, and combinatorial types organize an entire branch of discrete mathematics. This area underlies convex analysis, the Brunn-Minkowski theory, linear and integer programming geometry, and the classification of regular and semi-regular polytope families.
nodes.cs: Studuje geometrii konvexních množin v reálných vektorových prostorech — jejich metrické, afinní a kombinatorické invarianty (objem, smíšené objemy, opěrné funkce, Minkowského součty) — a specializuje se na mnohostěny a polytopy, konečně generovaná konvexní tělesa, jejichž svazy stěn, f-vektory a kombinatorické typy organizují celou větev diskrétní matematiky. Tato oblast je základem konvexní analýzy, Brunn-Minkowského teorie, geometrie lineárního a celočíselného programování a klasifikace pravidelných a polopravidelných rodin polytopů.
nodes.brief:
Studies the geometry of convex sets in real vector spaces — their metric, affine, and combinatorial invariants (volume, mixed volumes, support functions, Minkowski sums) — and specializes to polytopes and polyhedra, the finitely generated convex bodies whose face lattices, f-vectors, and combinatorial types organize an entire branch of discrete mathematics. This area underlies convex analysis, the Brunn-Minkowski theory, linear and integer programming geometry, and the classi

### Concepts
- **General convexity** (52Axx) — Studies convex sets and convex bodies in linear, affine, or metric spaces, including their support functions, mixed volumes, extremal problems, and inequalities such as Brunn-Minkowski.
- **Polytopes and polyhedra** (52Bxx) — Studies polytopes and polyhedra as combinatorial and geometric objects, analyzing their face lattices, f-vectors, symmetry groups, and realizability, including regular, convex, and abstract polytopes.

nodes.name: Discrete and Combinatorial Geometry
nodes.en: Investigates combinatorial properties of finite or discrete configurations of geometric objects — points, lines, circles, convex bodies — including incidence structures, arrangements, packings, coverings, and tilings. Central questions concern extremal counts (unit distances, incidences, k-sets), density bounds for sphere packing and lattice covering, and Helly-type and combinatorial convexity theorems; the area blends geometry with combinatorics and has strong ties to computational geometry.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá kombinatorické vlastnosti konečných či diskrétních konfigurací geometrických objektů — bodů, přímek, kružnic, konvexních těles — včetně incidenčních struktur, uspořádání, pakování, pokrytí a dláždění. Ústřední otázky se týkají extremálních počtů (jednotkové vzdálenosti, incidence, k-množiny), hustotních odhadů pro pakování koulí a mřížková pokrytí a vět Hellyho typu a kombinatorické konvexity; obor prolíná geometrii s kombinatorikou a má silné vazby na výpočetní geometrii.
nodes.brief:
Investigates combinatorial properties of finite or discrete configurations of geometric objects — points, lines, circles, convex bodies — including incidence structures, arrangements, packings, coverings, and tilings. Central questions concern extremal counts (unit distances, incidences, k-sets), density bounds for sphere packing and lattice covering, and Helly-type and combinatorial convexity theorems. The area blends geometry with combinatorics and has strong ties to comput

### Concepts
- **Discrete geometry** (52Cxx) — Studies combinatorial and metric properties of discrete configurations of points, lines, and convex bodies, including packings, coverings, tilings, arrangements, and incidence problems.

nodes.name: Differential geometry
nodes.en: Differential geometry studies smooth manifolds equipped with a metric, connection, or symplectic and contact structure, using calculus to analyze curvature, geodesics, and the local-to-global relationship between geometric and topological properties. It spans the classical theory of curves and surfaces, Riemannian and pseudo-Riemannian geometry, symplectic and contact geometry, and geometric evolution equations such as Ricci flow that deform metrics along curvature-driven flows.
nodes.cs: Diferenciální geometrie zkoumá hladké variety vybavené metrikou, konexí nebo symplektickou či kontaktní strukturou a pomocí diferenciálního počtu analyzuje křivost, geodetiky a vztah mezi lokálními a globálními geometrickými a topologickými vlastnostmi. Zahrnuje klasickou teorii křivek a ploch, Riemannovu a pseudoriemannovskou geometrii, symplektickou a kontaktní geometrii a geometrické evoluční rovnice, jako je Ricciho tok, které deformují metriku podél křivostí řízeného toku.
nodes.brief:
Differential geometry does calculus on curved spaces: manifolds with Riemannian or pseudo-Riemannian metrics, connections and curvature, geodesics, and symplectic and contact structures, from the classical theory of curves and surfaces to geometric evolution equations such as Ricci flow. The local-to-global question — how curvature constrains topology — is its defining tension.

Pseudo-Riemannian geometry is the mathematical body of general relativity in [[01.01]], and symplectic geometry that of Hamiltonian mechanics. The underlying manifold topology belongs to [[02.01.04.42]], the analytic machinery to [[02.01.04.43]], symmetry via Lie groups to [[02.01.04.17]], and variational methods — geodesics, minimal surfaces, curvature flows — to [[02.01.04.36]].

nodes.name: Riemannian, Classical & Symplectic-Contact Geometry
nodes.en: Studies the geometry of curves, surfaces, and manifolds equipped with a metric, connection, symplectic form, or contact structure, spanning the classical differential geometry of curves and surfaces, the global curvature-topology interplay of Riemannian and pseudo-Riemannian manifolds, and the phase-space geometry underlying Hamiltonian and contact mechanics. It supplies core tools reused across the field, including Frenet frames, geodesics, curvature tensors, and symplectomorphisms.
nodes.cs: Studuje geometrii křivek, ploch a variet vybavených metrikou, konexí, symplektickou formou nebo kontaktní strukturou – od klasické diferenciální geometrie křivek a ploch přes vztah křivosti a topologie u Riemannových a pseudoriemannovských variet až po fázový prostor hamiltonovské a kontaktní mechaniky. Poskytuje základní nástroje využívané napříč oborem, jako jsou Frenetovy repéry, geodetiky, tenzory křivosti a symplektomorfismy.
nodes.brief:
Studies the geometry of curves, surfaces, and manifolds equipped with a metric, connection, symplectic form, or contact structure, spanning the classical differential geometry of curves and surfaces through the global curvature-topology interplay of Riemannian and pseudo-Riemannian manifolds and the phase-space geometry underlying Hamiltonian and contact mechanics. It furnishes the foundational language used throughout modern geometry and mathematical physics: Frenet frames, 

### Concepts
- **Classical differential geometry** (53Axx) — Studies curves and surfaces in Euclidean, affine, and projective space using classical tools such as Frenet frames and the first and second fundamental forms.
- **Local differential geometry** (53Bxx) — Investigates the local structure of manifolds carrying a connection, metric, or other differential-geometric structure near a point.
- **Global differential geometry** (53Cxx) — Examines the global relationship between curvature and topology of Riemannian and pseudo-Riemannian manifolds through geodesics, comparison theorems, and holonomy.
- **Symplectic geometry, contact geometry** (53Dxx) — Studies manifolds equipped with symplectic, Poisson, or contact structures, the geometric setting for Hamiltonian and contact mechanics.

nodes.name: Geometric Evolution Equations & Applications
nodes.en: Covers partial differential equations that deform a geometric object such as a curve, surface, or Riemannian metric along a flow driven by curvature, including Ricci flow, mean curvature flow, and curve-shortening flow, together with the transfer of differential-geometric methods to the sciences and engineering. Geometric flows analyze long-time behavior and singularity formation, and are used to classify manifolds or optimize shapes, most famously in Perelman's resolution of the Poincare conjecture via Ricci flow.
nodes.cs: Zahrnuje parciální diferenciální rovnice, které deformují geometrický objekt – křivku, plochu nebo Riemannovu metriku – podél toku řízeného křivostí, včetně Ricciho toku, toku střední křivosti a toku zkracování křivek, spolu s přenosem diferenciálně-geometrických metod do přírodních a technických věd. Geometrické toky analyzují dlouhodobé chování a vznik singularit a slouží ke klasifikaci variet nebo optimalizaci tvarů, nejznáměji v Perelmanově důkazu Poincarého domněnky pomocí Ricciho toku.
nodes.brief:
Covers partial differential equations that deform a geometric object such as a curve, surface, or Riemannian metric along a flow driven by curvature, including Ricci flow, mean curvature flow, and curve-shortening flow, together with the transfer of differential-geometric methods to the sciences and engineering. Geometric flows analyze long-time behavior and singularity formation, and use flow-based techniques to classify manifolds or optimize shapes, most famously in the res

### Concepts
- **Geometric evolution equations** (53Exx) — Studies curvature-driven PDE flows of geometric objects, such as Ricci flow and mean curvature flow, and their long-time and singularity behavior.
- **Applications of differential geometry to sciences and engineering** (53Zxx) — Applies differential-geometric concepts and methods to problems in physics, biology, engineering, and related applied fields.

nodes.name: General topology
nodes.en: General topology develops the foundational vocabulary of point-set topology, including open and closed sets, convergence, continuity, compactness, connectedness, and separation properties, and studies how topological spaces are built, compared, and classified from these primitives. It underlies every other branch of topology, providing the common language in which algebraic, differential, and low-dimensional topology are formulated.
nodes.cs: Obecná topologie rozvíjí základní pojmový aparát bodové topologie – otevřené a uzavřené množiny, konvergenci, spojitost, kompaktnost, souvislost a separační axiomy – a zkoumá, jak jsou topologické prostory konstruovány, porovnávány a klasifikovány z těchto primitiv. Tvoří základ všech ostatních odvětví topologie a poskytuje společný jazyk, v němž se formulují algebraická, diferenciální i nízkodimenzionální topologie.
nodes.brief:
General topology is the discipline of the continuous at its most primitive: open sets, convergence of nets and filters, separation axioms, compactness, connectedness, metrization, and the constructions — products, quotients, hyperspaces — by which spaces are assembled. Counterexamples are part of its method: pathological spaces chart exactly how far each theorem reaches.

Every other topological pillar builds on it: the algebraic invariants of [[02.01.04.41]] and the manifolds of [[02.01.04.42]] presuppose its vocabulary, function spaces in [[02.01.04.34]] carry its topologies, topological dynamics in [[02.01.04.26]] iterates its maps, and set-theoretic topology keeps a working channel open to [[02.01.04.03]].

nodes.name: Foundations and Basic Constructions
nodes.en: Establishes the primitive vocabulary of topology — open sets, convergence of nets and filters, comparison of topologies — and the standard set-theoretic operations by which new spaces are built from old, such as subspaces, products, quotients, and hyperspaces. This is the settled bedrock on which every other area of general topology depends.
nodes.cs: Ustavuje základní slovník topologie — otevřené množiny, konvergenci sítí a filtrů, porovnávání topologií — a standardní množinové operace, jimiž se z prostorů budují nové: podprostory, součiny, faktorové prostory a hyperprostory. Jde o ustálené podloží, na němž závisí každá další oblast obecné topologie.
nodes.brief:
Establishes the primitive vocabulary of topology — open sets, convergence of nets and filters, comparison of topologies — and the standard set-theoretic operations by which new spaces are built from old, such as subspaces, products, quotients, and hyperspaces. This is the settled bedrock on which every other area of general topology depends.

### Concepts
- **Generalities in topology** (54Axx) — Studies the foundational axioms and basic notions of topological spaces, including comparison of topologies and convergence of nets and filters.
- **Basic constructions in general topology** (54Bxx) — Develops the standard operations—subspaces, products, quotients, disjoint sums, and hyperspaces—used to build new topological spaces from given ones.

nodes.name: Maps and Classification of Topological Spaces
nodes.en: Studies how continuous, open, closed, and perfect maps shape and relate topological spaces, and organizes spaces both by broad structural invariants (separation axioms, compactness and connectedness variants, covering properties) and by finer special properties such as dimension and orderability. This classificatory core of general topology is classical textbook material, largely settled since the mid-20th century.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá, jak spojitá, otevřená, uzavřená a perfektní zobrazení formují topologické prostory a vztahy mezi nimi, a třídí prostory jednak podle širokých strukturních invariantů (oddělovací axiomy, varianty kompaktnosti a souvislosti, pokrývací vlastnosti), jednak podle jemnějších zvláštních vlastností, jako jsou dimenze a uspořádatelnost. Toto klasifikační jádro obecné topologie je klasickou učebnicovou látkou, v podstatě uzavřenou od poloviny 20. století.
nodes.brief:
Studies how continuous, open, closed, and perfect maps shape and relate topological spaces, and organizes spaces both by broad structural invariants (separation axioms, compactness and connectedness variants, covering properties) and by finer special properties such as dimension and orderability. This classificatory core of general topology is classical textbook material, largely settled since the mid-20th century.

### Concepts
- **Maps and general types of topological spaces defined by maps** (54Cxx) — Classifies topological spaces via distinguished classes of continuous, open, closed, perfect, and quotient maps, such as images of metric or compact spaces.
- **Fairly general properties of topological spaces** (54Dxx) — Examines coarse structural properties—separation axioms, compactness-type conditions, connectedness, and covering properties—used to classify spaces broadly.
- **Special properties of topological spaces** (54Fxx) — Treats finer structural invariants, including dimension theory, continua theory, and orderability, that distinguish narrower classes of spaces.

nodes.name: Enriched Structures, Exotic Spaces, and Applications
nodes.en: Enriches bare topological spaces with auxiliary structures such as uniformities, proximities, and metrics; catalogues counterexamples and pathological spaces that mark the boundaries of general theorems; and connects topology to algebra, order theory, dynamics, and nonstandard analysis. The uniform-structure and counterexample theory is classical, while the study of applications and infinitesimal (nonstandard) reformulations remains an active, comparatively niche frontier.
nodes.cs: Obohacuje holé topologické prostory o pomocné struktury, jako jsou uniformity, proximity a metriky; katalogizuje protipříklady a patologické prostory vyznačující hranice obecných vět; a propojuje topologii s algebrou, teorií uspořádání, dynamikou a nestandardní analýzou. Teorie uniformních struktur a protipříkladů je klasická, zatímco studium aplikací a infinitezimálních (nestandardních) reformulací zůstává živou, poměrně úzkou frontou.
nodes.brief:
Enriches bare topological spaces with auxiliary structures such as uniformities, proximities, and metrics; catalogues counterexamples and pathological spaces that mark the boundaries of general theorems; and connects topology to algebra, order theory, dynamics, and nonstandard analysis. The uniform-structure and counterexample theory is classical, while the study of applications and infinitesimal (nonstandard) reformulations remains an active, comparatively niche frontier.

### Concepts
- **Topological spaces with richer structures** (54Exx) — Studies spaces endowed with auxiliary structures—metrics, uniformities, proximities, and quasi-uniformities—that refine purely topological data.
- **Peculiar topological spaces** (54Gxx) — Catalogues counterexamples and pathological spaces exhibiting unusual combinations of topological properties, delimiting the scope of general theorems.
- **Connections of general topology with other structures, applications** (54Hxx) — Explores interactions between topology and algebraic, order-theoretic, and dynamical structures, including topological groups and topological dynamics, and applications beyond topology proper.
- **Nonstandard topology** (54Jxx) — Applies nonstandard analysis and infinitesimal methods to reformulate topological notions such as convergence, compactness, and continuity.

nodes.name: Algebraic topology
nodes.en: Algebraic topology studies topological spaces by assigning them algebraic invariants, such as homotopy groups, homology and cohomology theories, and characteristic classes, that are preserved under continuous deformation. These invariants translate geometric questions about spaces and continuous maps into computable problems in group theory, ring theory, and homological algebra.
nodes.cs: Algebraická topologie studuje topologické prostory přiřazováním algebraických invariantů – homotopických grup, homologických a kohomologických teorií a charakteristických tříd –, které jsou zachovány při spojité deformaci. Tyto invarianty převádějí geometrické otázky o prostorech a spojitých zobrazeních na řešitelné problémy teorie grup, teorie okruhů a homologické algebry.
nodes.brief:
Algebraic topology converts spaces into algebra: homotopy and homology groups, cohomology rings, characteristic classes and spectra assign to each space invariants that continuous deformation cannot change, turning geometric distinctions into computable algebraic ones. Obstruction theory and spectral sequences are its heavy machinery.

It gave birth to the category theory and homological algebra of [[02.01.04.14]] and to the K-theory of [[02.01.04.15]]; its point-set substrate is [[02.01.04.40]] and its favorite objects — manifolds and CW complexes — live in [[02.01.04.42]]; fundamental groups tie it to [[02.01.04.16]], sheaf cohomology to [[02.01.04.10]], and topological quantum field theories to the mathematical frontier of [[01.01]].

nodes.name: Homology, Homotopy, and Classical Invariants
nodes.en: Covers the core invariants of algebraic topology: singular, simplicial, and generalized (co)homology theories, homotopy groups and homotopy types, and the classical fixed-point, degree, and duality theorems built from them. These tools assign algebraic invariants, such as groups, rings, and homotopy classes of maps, to topological spaces in a way that is functorial and computable, forming the load-bearing machinery used throughout geometry, manifold theory, and mathematical physics.
nodes.cs: Zahrnuje základní invarianty algebraické topologie: singulární, simpliciální a zobecněné (ko)homologické teorie, homotopické grupy a homotopické typy a klasické věty o pevném bodu, stupni zobrazení a dualitě, které z nich vycházejí. Tyto nástroje přiřazují topologickým prostorům algebraické invarianty – grupy, okruhy a homotopické třídy zobrazení – funktoriálním a vypočitatelným způsobem a tvoří nosnou aparaturu geometrie, teorie variet i matematické fyziky.
nodes.brief:
Covers the core invariants of algebraic topology: singular, simplicial, and generalized (co)homology theories, homotopy groups and homotopy types, and the classical fixed-point, degree, and duality theorems built from them. These tools assign algebraic invariants — groups, rings, and homotopy classes of maps — to topological spaces in a way that is functorial and computable, forming the load-bearing machinery used throughout geometry, manifold theory, and mathematical physics

### Concepts
- **Classical topics in algebraic topology** (55Mxx) — Studies classical invariants and theorems such as fixed-point theory, degree theory, duality, and covering-space arguments applied to topological spaces.
- **Homology and cohomology theories in algebraic topology** (55Nxx) — Develops singular, simplicial, Čech, and generalized (co)homology theories, together with the axiomatic and computational frameworks that relate them.
- **Homotopy theory** (55Pxx) — Studies spaces and maps up to homotopy equivalence via CW complexes, model categories, localization, and the classification of homotopy types.
- **Homotopy groups** (55Qxx) — Computes and organizes homotopy groups of spaces, including stable homotopy groups of spheres and homotopy groups of classifying spaces and Lie groups.

nodes.name: Bundles, Operations, and Homological Machinery
nodes.en: Assembles the structural and computational apparatus that operates on top of homotopy and homology: fiber bundles and their classifying spaces, cohomology operations and obstruction theory, spectral sequences, and the categorical and homological-algebra framework, including derived categories, model structures, and operads, underlying modern algebraic topology. These tools let topologists extract fine invariants from complex fibered or filtered spaces and organize computations too large to handle by direct methods.
nodes.cs: Shromažďuje strukturální a výpočetní aparát nadstavěný nad homotopií a homologií: vláknové svazky a jejich klasifikující prostory, kohomologické operace a teorii obstrukcí, spektrální posloupnosti a kategoriální a homologicko-algebraický rámec – odvozené kategorie, modelové struktury, operády –, na němž stojí moderní algebraická topologie. Tyto nástroje umožňují topologům získávat jemné invarianty z komplikovaných vláknových či filtrovaných prostorů a zvládat výpočty příliš rozsáhlé pro přímé metody.
nodes.brief:
Assembles the structural and computational apparatus that operates on top of homotopy and homology: fiber bundles and their classifying spaces, cohomology operations and obstruction theory, spectral sequences, and the categorical/homological-algebra framework (derived categories, model structures, operads) underlying modern algebraic topology. These tools let topologists extract fine invariants from complex fibered or filtered spaces and organize large computations that would

### Concepts
- **Fiber spaces and bundles in algebraic topology** (55Rxx) — Studies fiber bundles, fibrations, and their classifying spaces, including characteristic classes that measure their twisting.
- **Operations and obstructions in algebraic topology** (55Sxx) — Develops cohomology operations (such as Steenrod squares) and obstruction theory for extending or lifting maps across cell complexes.
- **Spectral sequences in algebraic topology** (55Txx) — Constructs and applies spectral sequences (Serre, Adams, Atiyah-Hirzebruch, etc.) as filtered computational tools for homology and homotopy.
- **Applied homological algebra and category theory in algebraic topology** (55Uxx) — Applies simplicial sets, model categories, derived functors, and operadic structures as the categorical foundation for homotopical algebra.

nodes.name: Manifolds and cell complexes
nodes.en: Manifolds and cell complexes studies spaces that are locally modeled on Euclidean space or built from combinatorial building blocks, such as CW and simplicial complexes, and classifies them up to homeomorphism, diffeomorphism, or piecewise-linear equivalence. It ranges from low-dimensional manifolds, knots, and surfaces, through smooth structures and transformation groups, to applications of topology in data analysis and the sciences.
nodes.cs: Variety a buněčné komplexy zkoumají prostory lokálně modelované euklidovským prostorem nebo sestavené z kombinatorických stavebních bloků – CW a simpliciálních komplexů – a klasifikují je až na homeomorfismus, difeomorfismus nebo po částech lineární ekvivalenci. Obor sahá od nízkodimenzionálních variet, uzlů a ploch přes hladké struktury a transformační grupy až po aplikace topologie v analýze dat a přírodních vědách.
nodes.brief:
Manifolds — spaces locally like Euclidean space — and the cell complexes from which they are assembled, classified up to homeomorphism, diffeomorphism or piecewise-linear equivalence. Low dimensions are the pillar's dramatic stage: knots and links, surfaces, 3-manifolds after geometrization, and the exotic phenomena of dimension four.

Its invariants come from the algebraic topology of [[02.01.04.41]] over the point-set base of [[02.01.04.40]]; smooth structures and geometric methods are shared with [[02.01.04.39]] and the analysis of [[02.01.04.43]]; quantum and knot invariants link it to the Hopf-algebraic side of [[02.01.04.12]] and to [[01.01]]; and topological data analysis carries its tools into applied science alongside the structured-data statistics of [[02.01.04.45]].

nodes.name: Low-Dimensional Topology
nodes.en: Studies the classification and structure of manifolds in dimensions two through four, where the tools of surgery theory and general position fail and phenomena diverge sharply from the high-dimensional world. Central objects include knots, links, surfaces, and 3- and 4-manifolds, studied through combinatorial, geometric, and increasingly gauge-theoretic and Floer-theoretic invariants. The 3-dimensional case was largely resolved by Thurston's geometrization program and Perelman's proof of the Poincare conjecture, while dimension four remains comparatively open.
nodes.cs: Studuje klasifikaci a strukturu variet ve dvou až čtyřech dimenzích, kde selhávají nástroje chirurgické teorie a obecné polohy a jevy se výrazně odlišují od vysokodimenzionálního světa. Ústředními objekty jsou uzly, zápletky, plochy a 3- a 4-variety, zkoumané kombinatorickými, geometrickými a stále více kalibračně-teoretickými a Floerovými invarianty. Trojrozměrný případ byl z velké části vyřešen Thurstonovým geometrizačním programem a Perelmanovým důkazem Poincarého domněnky, zatímco čtyřrozměrný případ zůstává poměrně otevřený.
nodes.brief:
Studies the classification and structure of manifolds in dimensions two through four, where the tools of surgery theory and general position fail and phenomena diverge sharply from the high-dimensional world. Central objects include knots, links, surfaces, and 3- and 4-manifolds, studied through combinatorial, geometric, and increasingly gauge-theoretic and Floer-theoretic invariants. The 3-dimensional case was largely resolved by Thurston's geometrization program and Perelma

### Concepts
- **Low-dimensional topology in specific dimensions** (57Kxx) — Studies knots, links, and 3- and 4-manifolds via dimension-specific invariants such as Jones polynomials, Khovanov homology, and Heegaard Floer theory.
- **General low-dimensional topology** (57Mxx) — Develops general combinatorial and group-theoretic techniques for surfaces and 3-manifolds, including fundamental groups, covering spaces, and Heegaard splittings.

nodes.name: Manifold Structures and PL-Topology
nodes.en: Compares the principal categories in which manifolds can be studied, namely topological (up to homeomorphism), piecewise-linear (up to PL-homeomorphism), and generalized homology manifolds, and asks when structures in one category can be found, classified, or triangulated in another. Surgery theory, the Hauptvermutung, and the triangulation conjecture (resolved negatively by Manolescu in 2013 using Pin(2)-equivariant Seiberg-Witten theory) are the organizing themes; the classical smooth and PL cases are largely settled, while triangulability of higher-dimensional manifolds remains an active research frontier.
nodes.cs: Porovnává hlavní kategorie, v nichž lze variety studovat – topologickou (až na homeomorfismus), po částech lineární (až na PL-homeomorfismus) a zobecněné homologické variety – a zkoumá, kdy lze struktury v jedné kategorii nalézt, klasifikovat nebo triangulovat v jiné. Ústředními tématy jsou chirurgická teorie, Hauptvermutung a domněnka o triangulovatelnosti (vyvrácená Manolescuem v roce 2013 pomocí Pin(2)-ekvivariantní Seibergovy–Wittenovy teorie); klasické hladké a PL případy jsou z velké části vyřešeny, zatímco triangulovatelnost vysokodimenzionálních variet zůstává aktivní výzkumnou frontou.
nodes.brief:
Compares the principal categories in which manifolds can be studied — topological (up to homeomorphism), piecewise-linear (up to PL-homeomorphism), and generalized homology manifolds — and asks when structures in one category can be found, classified, or triangulated in another. Surgery theory, the Hauptvermutung, and the triangulation conjecture (resolved negatively by Manolescu in 2013 using Pin(2)-equivariant Seiberg-Witten theory) are the organizing themes. The classical 

### Concepts
- **Topological manifolds** (57Nxx) — Classifies manifolds up to homeomorphism and studies their embeddings and characterizations, chiefly via surgery theory and the Hauptvermutung.
- **Generalized manifolds** (57Pxx) — Studies spaces such as ANR homology manifolds that share the local cohomological properties of manifolds without necessarily being genuine manifolds.
- **PL-topology** (57Qxx) — Studies manifolds and complexes with a piecewise-linear structure, including triangulations, PL classification, and their relation to the smooth and topological categories.

nodes.name: Differential Topology and Transformation Groups
nodes.en: Studies smooth manifold structures, such as exotic smoothings, cobordism, and characteristic classes, alongside the groups and homotopy-theoretic structures that act on or model manifolds. Group actions by diffeomorphisms or homeomorphisms, their fixed-point sets and quotients, and the homology and homotopy theory of the acting Lie or topological groups are treated as a unified package, since transformation-group problems routinely reduce to differential-topological ones and vice versa.
nodes.cs: Studuje struktury hladkých variet – exotická vyhlazení, kobordismy a charakteristické třídy – spolu s grupami a homotopicko-teoretickými strukturami, které na varietách působí nebo je modelují. Grupové akce difeomorfismy či homeomorfismy, jejich pevné body a kvocienty a homologická a homotopická teorie působících Lieho či topologických grup jsou pojímány jako jednotný celek, neboť problémy transformačních grup se běžně převádějí na diferenciálně-topologické a naopak.
nodes.brief:
Studies smooth manifold structures — exotic smoothings, cobordism, and characteristic classes — alongside the groups and homotopy-theoretic structures that act on or model manifolds. Group actions by diffeomorphisms or homeomorphisms, their fixed-point sets and quotients, and the homology and homotopy theory of the acting Lie or topological groups are treated as a unified package, since transformation-group problems routinely reduce to differential-topological ones and vice v

### Concepts
- **Differential topology** (57Rxx) — Studies differentiable manifolds up to diffeomorphism, including exotic smooth structures, cobordism theory, characteristic classes, and smoothing theory.
- **Topological transformation groups** (57Sxx) — Studies group actions on manifolds by homeomorphisms or diffeomorphisms, together with their classification, fixed-point sets, and orbit spaces.
- **Homology and homotopy of topological groups and related structures** (57Txx) — Studies the homology and homotopy theory of topological and Lie groups, homogeneous spaces, and their classifying spaces.

nodes.name: Manifolds and Topology in Science and Engineering
nodes.en: Applies manifold theory, cell complexes, and low-dimensional topology to problems arising outside pure mathematics, including topological data analysis, configuration spaces in robotics, and knot-theoretic models in physics and molecular biology. This is a young interface field where classical topological invariants are repurposed as practical descriptors of shape, connectivity, and constraint in applied data.
nodes.cs: Aplikuje teorii variet, buněčné komplexy a nízkodimenzionální topologii na problémy mimo čistou matematiku, včetně topologické analýzy dat, konfiguračních prostorů v robotice a uzlových modelů ve fyzice a molekulární biologii. Jde o mladý hraniční obor, v němž se klasické topologické invarianty přetvářejí v praktické deskriptory tvaru, konektivity a vazeb v aplikovaných datech.
nodes.brief:
Applies manifold theory, cell complexes, and low-dimensional topology to problems arising outside pure mathematics, including topological data analysis, configuration spaces in robotics, and knot-theoretic models in physics and molecular biology. This is a young interface field where classical topological invariants are repurposed as practical descriptors of shape, connectivity, and constraint in applied data.

### Concepts
- **Relations of manifolds and cell complexes with science and engineering** (57Zxx) — Covers applications of manifold and cell-complex theory to physics, engineering, robotics, and data science, including topological data analysis and configuration-space models.

nodes.name: Global analysis, analysis on manifolds
nodes.en: Global analysis, analysis on manifolds is the branch of mathematics studying differential and variational structures defined intrinsically on smooth and infinite-dimensional manifolds — spanning the foundational theory of manifolds and mapping spaces, the calculus of nonlinear operators and variational problems posed on them, and the partial differential equations, index theory and singularity classification built on top of that machinery. Geometric integration theory is classified separately, under measure and integration.
nodes.cs: Globální analýza, analýza na varietách je obor matematiky zkoumající diferenciální a variační struktury definované vnitřně na hladkých a nekonečně-dimenzionálních varietách — zahrnuje základní teorii variet a prostorů zobrazení, kalkulus nelineárních operátorů a variační problémy na nich formulované i parciální diferenciální rovnice, teorii indexu a klasifikaci singularit na tomto aparátu vystavěné. Teorie geometrické integrace je klasifikována samostatně, pod teorií míry a integrálu.
nodes.brief:
Global analysis does analysis where the domain itself is a manifold, possibly infinite-dimensional: manifolds of mappings, nonlinear operators and variational problems posed intrinsically, elliptic operators with index theory and heat-kernel methods, and the singularity theory of smooth maps up to catastrophe theory.

The Atiyah-Singer index theorem is its emblem, fusing the operator theory of [[02.01.04.35]], the K-theory of [[02.01.04.15]] and the geometry of [[02.01.04.39]] over the manifolds of [[02.01.04.42]]. PDE technique flows in from [[02.01.04.25]], variational structure from [[02.01.04.36]], and gauge theory with moduli-space methods keeps it on the mathematical front line of [[01.01]].

nodes.name: Foundations: Manifolds, Infinite-Dimensional Spaces, and General Structures
nodes.en: Establishes the basic differentiable-manifold framework that global analysis builds on, including its extension to infinite-dimensional model spaces (Banach and Fréchet manifolds) and the generalized geometric structures — pseudogroups, Lie groupoids, foliation-type data — that go beyond the classical finite-dimensional, Lie-group-acted manifold. This layer supplies the ambient objects on which calculus, operator theory and PDE are subsequently developed.
nodes.cs: Zakládá základní rámec diferencovatelných variet, na němž staví globální analýza, včetně jeho rozšíření na nekonečně-dimenzionální modelové prostory (Banachovy a Fréchetovy variety) a zobecněné geometrické struktury — pseudogrupy, Lieovy grupoidy, foliační data — jdoucí za klasickou konečně-dimenzionální varietu s působením Lieovy grupy. Tato vrstva poskytuje objekty, na nichž se následně buduje kalkulus, teorie operátorů a parciální diferenciální rovnice.
nodes.brief:
Establishes the basic differentiable-manifold framework that global analysis builds on, including its extension to infinite-dimensional model spaces (Banach/Frechet manifolds) and the generalized geometric structures - pseudogroups, groupoids, foliation-type data - that go beyond the classical finite-dimensional Lie-group-acted manifold. This layer supplies the ambient objects on which calculus, operator theory, and PDE are subsequently developed. It sits at the interface wit

### Concepts
- **General theory of differentiable manifolds** (58Axx) — Develops the basic definitions, categories, and structural results for smooth (finite-dimensional) manifolds underlying global analysis.
- **Infinite-dimensional manifolds** (58Bxx) — Extends manifold theory to Banach, Hilbert, and Frechet model spaces, enabling analysis on function spaces and gauge-theoretic configuration spaces.
- **Pseudogroups, differentiable groupoids and general structures on manifolds** (58Hxx) — Studies pseudogroups, Lie groupoids, and other generalized transformation structures that encode local symmetries and foliated geometry on manifolds.

nodes.name: Calculus on Manifolds, Mapping Spaces, and Variational Problems
nodes.en: Covers nonlinear analysis intrinsic to manifolds: differential calculus of nonlinear operators, the geometry of spaces of mappings between manifolds (infinite-dimensional analogues of function spaces), and variational problems posed in these infinite-dimensional settings, such as harmonic maps and minimal submanifolds arising as critical points of geometric functionals. It is the technical engine behind global existence results in geometric analysis and Morse theory.
nodes.cs: Zahrnuje nelineární analýzu vlastní varietám: diferenciální kalkulus nelineárních operátorů, geometrii prostorů zobrazení mezi varietami (nekonečně-dimenzionální obdoby prostorů funkcí) a variační problémy formulované v těchto nekonečně-dimenzionálních prostředích, jako jsou harmonická zobrazení a minimální podvariety vznikající jako kritické body geometrických funkcionálů. Je technickým motorem výsledků o globální existenci v geometrické analýze a Morseově teorii.
nodes.brief:
Covers nonlinear analysis intrinsic to manifolds: differential calculus of nonlinear operators, the geometry of spaces and manifolds built from mappings between manifolds (infinite-dimensional analogues of function spaces), and variational problems posed in these infinite-dimensional settings, such as harmonic maps and minimal submanifolds obtained as critical points of geometric functionals. It is the technical engine behind global existence results in geometric analysis, co

### Concepts
- **Calculus on manifolds; nonlinear operators** (58Cxx) — Studies differentiation, integration, and nonlinear operator theory (e.g. Fredholm maps, degree theory) for mappings between manifolds.
- **Spaces and manifolds of mappings** (58Dxx) — Investigates the topology and differentiable structure of spaces of mappings between manifolds, generalizing function-space theory to a nonlinear, infinite-dimensional setting.
- **Variational problems in infinite-dimensional spaces** (58Exx) — Analyzes critical points of functionals on infinite-dimensional manifolds, including geodesics, harmonic maps, minimal surfaces, and Morse-theoretic methods.

nodes.name: PDE, Singularity Theory, and Applications of Global Analysis
nodes.en: Addresses partial differential equations and differential operators defined intrinsically on manifolds (elliptic operators, index theory, heat-kernel methods), the classification of singularities of smooth maps via catastrophe theory, and the deployment of these global-analytic tools to problems arising in physics and the other sciences. This block links geometric analysis to spectral theory, bifurcation phenomena, and mathematical physics.
nodes.cs: Věnuje se parciálním diferenciálním rovnicím a diferenciálním operátorům definovaným vnitřně na varietách (eliptické operátory, teorie indexu, metody tepelného jádra), klasifikaci singularit hladkých zobrazení pomocí teorie katastrof a nasazení těchto globálně-analytických nástrojů na problémy fyziky a dalších věd. Tento blok spojuje geometrickou analýzu se spektrální teorií, bifurkačními jevy a matematickou fyzikou.
nodes.brief:
Addresses partial differential equations and differential operators defined intrinsically on manifolds (elliptic operators, index theory, heat-kernel methods), the classification of singularities of smooth maps via catastrophe theory, and the deployment of these global-analytic tools to problems arising in physics and the other sciences. This block links geometric analysis to spectral theory, bifurcation phenomena, and mathematical physics.

### Concepts
- **Partial differential equations on manifolds; differential operators** (58Jxx) — Studies elliptic and other differential operators and PDEs formulated intrinsically on manifolds, including index theory and heat-kernel asymptotics.
- **Theory of singularities and catastrophe theory** (58Kxx) — Classifies singularities of smooth maps and unfoldings of critical points, forming the mathematical basis of catastrophe theory.
- **Applications of global analysis to the sciences** (58Zxx) — Applies manifold-based analytic techniques to problems in physics and other sciences, such as gauge theory and dynamical modeling.

nodes.name: Probability theory and stochastic processes
nodes.en: Probability theory and stochastic processes is the branch of mathematics modeling randomness and its evolution over time — from measure-theoretic foundations, distribution theory and limit theorems, through probability on structured and geometric spaces, to the general theory of stochastic and Markov processes and the stochastic and rough-path calculus built on top of them.
nodes.cs: Teorie pravděpodobnosti a stochastické procesy je obor matematiky modelující náhodnost a její vývoj v čase — od měrově-teoretických základů, teorie rozdělení a limitních vět přes pravděpodobnost na strukturovaných a geometrických prostorech až po obecnou teorii stochastických a Markovových procesů a na nich vystavěný stochastický kalkulus a teorii hrubých drah.
nodes.brief:
Probability theory is measure theory animated by interpretation: random variables, distributions and limit theorems on the axiomatic base of [[02.01.04.19]], then stochastic processes — Markov chains, martingales, Brownian motion — and the stochastic and rough-path calculus that integrates against randomness itself.

It is the theoretical substrate of the statistics in [[02.01.04.45]] and the stochastic finance of [[02.01.04.49]]; ergodic theory in [[02.01.04.26]] is its dynamical twin; probabilistic potential theory answers to [[02.01.04.21]]; the probabilistic method serves [[02.01.04.04]]; and through statistical mechanics, percolation and stochastic PDEs it works the mathematical frontier of [[01.01]].

nodes.name: Foundations, Distribution Theory, and Limit Theorems
nodes.en: Comprises the axiomatic and analytic core of probability: measure-theoretic foundations, the theory of distributions and characteristic functions, combinatorial probability on discrete structures, and the asymptotic laws — laws of large numbers, central limit theorems, large deviations — governing sums and sequences of random variables. This is the settled bedrock on which every other area of the discipline builds, codified in the classical textbooks of Kolmogorov, Feller and Loève.
nodes.cs: Zahrnuje axiomatické a analytické jádro pravděpodobnosti: měrově-teoretické základy, teorii rozdělení a charakteristických funkcí, kombinatorickou pravděpodobnost na diskrétních strukturách a asymptotické zákony — zákony velkých čísel, centrální limitní věty, teorii velkých odchylek — řídící součty a posloupnosti náhodných veličin. Jde o ustálený základ, na němž staví každá další oblast oboru, kodifikovaný v klasických učebnicích Kolmogorova, Fellera a Loèva.
nodes.brief:
Comprises the axiomatic and analytic core of probability: measure-theoretic foundations, the theory of distributions and characteristic functions, combinatorial probability on discrete structures, and the asymptotic laws — laws of large numbers, central limit theorems, large deviations — governing sums and sequences of random variables. This is the settled bedrock on which every other area of the discipline builds, codified in the classical textbooks of Kolmogorov, Feller, an

### Concepts
- **Foundations of probability theory** (60Axx) — Establishes the measure-theoretic axioms, probability spaces, and general conceptual apparatus underlying the rest of probability theory.
- **Combinatorial probability** (60Cxx) — Applies probabilistic reasoning to discrete combinatorial structures, including random graphs, permutations, urn schemes, and occupancy problems.
- **Distribution theory** (60Exx) — Studies the analytic properties of probability distributions — characteristic functions, infinite divisibility, moments, and distributional inequalities.
- **Limit theorems in probability theory** (60Fxx) — Characterizes the asymptotic behavior of sequences and sums of random variables via laws of large numbers, central limit theorems, and large-deviation principles.

nodes.name: Probability on Structured and Geometric Spaces
nodes.en: Extends probability beyond the real line to random elements valued in topological groups, Banach spaces and other algebraic structures, and to geometric probability, which studies random points, sets and shapes embedded in Euclidean space or on manifolds. Random walks on groups, invariance principles in infinite dimensions, and stochastic geometry's random tessellations and point processes tie analysis, algebra and geometry into a common probabilistic language.
nodes.cs: Rozšiřuje pravděpodobnost za reálnou osu na náhodné prvky s hodnotami v topologických grupách, Banachových prostorech a dalších algebraických strukturách a na geometrickou pravděpodobnost, jež zkoumá náhodné body, množiny a tvary vložené do eukleidovského prostoru nebo na varietách. Náhodné procházky na grupách, principy invariance v nekonečné dimenzi a náhodné teselace a bodové procesy stochastické geometrie propojují analýzu, algebru a geometrii do společného pravděpodobnostního jazyka.
nodes.brief:
Extends probability beyond the real line to random elements valued in topological groups, Banach spaces, and other algebraic structures, and to geometric probability, which studies random points, sets, and shapes embedded in Euclidean space or on manifolds. Random walks on groups, invariance principles in infinite dimensions, and stochastic geometry's random tessellations and point processes tie analysis, algebra, and geometry into a common probabilistic language. The area si

### Concepts
- **Probability theory on algebraic and topological structures** (60Bxx) — Studies probability measures and random elements on groups, vector spaces, and other algebraic or topological structures, including weak convergence in such settings.
- **Geometric probability and stochastic geometry** (60Dxx) — Analyzes random geometric objects — points, sets, lines, and tessellations — and the probabilistic laws governing their configuration and interaction.

nodes.name: Stochastic Processes, Markov Theory, and Special Models
nodes.en: Develops the general theory of stochastic processes — martingales, Gaussian and stationary processes, stopping times — together with Markov processes, whose transition structure links probability to potential theory and partial differential equations, and the special process families (branching, renewal, queueing) that model discrete stochastic evolution in applications. Markov process theory remains a deep and actively researched bridge between probability and analysis.
nodes.cs: Rozvíjí obecnou teorii stochastických procesů — martingaly, Gaussovské a stacionární procesy, časy zastavení — spolu s Markovovými procesy, jejichž přechodová struktura propojuje pravděpodobnost s teorií potenciálu a parciálními diferenciálními rovnicemi, a se speciálními rodinami procesů (větvící, obnovovací, hromadné obsluhy) modelujícími diskrétní stochastický vývoj v aplikacích. Teorie Markovových procesů zůstává hlubokým a aktivně zkoumaným mostem mezi pravděpodobností a analýzou.
nodes.brief:
Develops the general theory of stochastic processes — martingales, Gaussian and stationary processes, stopping times — together with Markov processes, whose transition structure links probability to potential theory and partial differential equations, and the special process families (branching, renewal, queueing) that model discrete stochastic evolution in applications. Markov process theory remains a deep and actively researched bridge between probability and analysis, whil

### Concepts
- **Stochastic processes** (60Gxx) — Provides the general theory of families of random variables indexed by time or space, covering martingales, Gaussian processes, and stationarity.
- **Markov processes** (60Jxx) — Studies processes whose future evolution depends on the present state alone, including Markov chains, diffusions, and their transition semigroups.
- **Special processes** (60Kxx) — Covers specialized stochastic process models arising in applications, such as renewal processes, queueing systems, and branching processes.

nodes.name: Stochastic Calculus and Rough Analysis
nodes.en: Concerns the calculus built atop stochastic processes: Itô and Malliavin stochastic calculus, stochastic differential and partial differential equations, and the rough-path and regularity-structures framework that gives meaning to equations driven by highly irregular noise. Stochastic analysis is a mature field underpinning mathematical finance and SPDE theory, while rough analysis, launched by Lyons in the late 1990s and extended by Hairer's regularity structures, handles noise too rough for classical Itô calculus.
nodes.cs: Týká se kalkulu vystavěného na stochastických procesech: Itova a Malliavinova stochastického kalkulu, stochastických diferenciálních a parciálních diferenciálních rovnic a teorie hrubých drah (rough paths) a regularitních struktur (regularity structures), jež dává smysl rovnicím řízeným vysoce nepravidelným šumem. Stochastická analýza je vyzrálý obor stojící za matematickými financemi a teorií SPDE, zatímco teorii hrubých drah, kterou v pozdních 90. letech založil Lyons a rozšířil Hairerovou teorií regularitních struktur, zvládá šum příliš nepravidelný pro klasický Itův kalkulus.
nodes.brief:
Concerns the calculus built atop stochastic processes: Itô and Malliavin stochastic calculus, stochastic differential and partial differential equations, and the pathwise rough-path and regularity-structures framework that gives meaning to equations driven by highly irregular noise. Stochastic analysis is a mature, deeply developed field underpinning mathematical finance and SPDE theory, while rough analysis, launched by Lyons in the late 1990s and extended by Hairer's regula

### Concepts
- **Stochastic analysis** (60Hxx) — Develops stochastic calculus — stochastic integrals, differential equations, and Malliavin calculus — for processes driven by Brownian motion and related noises.
- **Rough analysis** (60Lxx) — Constructs a pathwise theory of differential equations driven by irregular signals via rough paths and regularity structures, independent of probabilistic averaging.

nodes.name: Statistics
nodes.en: Statistics is the mathematical discipline of drawing inference from data — spanning the foundations of inference and decision theory, sampling and distribution theory, parametric and nonparametric estimation and testing, multivariate analysis and regression, experimental design, and inference for stochastic and survival processes, together with their algebraic and topological extensions and applied reference methods.
nodes.cs: Statistika je matematická disciplína vyvozování závěrů z dat — zahrnuje základy inference a teorii rozhodování, teorii výběrů a rozdělení, parametrickou a neparametrickou estimaci a testování, vícerozměrnou analýzu a regresi, plánování experimentů a inferenci pro stochastické a přežívací procesy spolu s jejich algebraickými a topologickými rozšířeními a aplikovanými referenčními metodami.
nodes.brief:
Statistics inverts probability: given data, infer the mechanism.
